**Toronto**[[c]](#cite_note-15) is the [most populous city in Canada](/wiki/List_of_the_largest_municipalities_in_Canada_by_population "List of the largest municipalities in Canada by population") and the [capital city](/wiki/Capital_city "Capital city") of the [Canadian province](/wiki/Provinces_and_territories_of_Canada "Provinces and territories of Canada") of [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario"). With a population of 2,794,356 in 2021,[[13]](#cite_note-:2-16) the city is the [fourth-most populous city in North America](/wiki/List_of_North_American_cities_by_population "List of North American cities by population"), after [Mexico City](/wiki/Mexico_City "Mexico City"), [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), and [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles"). Toronto is the anchor of the [Golden Horseshoe](/wiki/Golden_Horseshoe "Golden Horseshoe"), an urban agglomeration surrounding the western end of [Lake Ontario](/wiki/Lake_Ontario "Lake Ontario") with an estimated population of 11,198,136 in 2025.[[14]](#cite_note-17)[[15]](#cite_note-18) As of 2025, the Toronto [census metropolitan area](/wiki/Census_metropolitan_area "Census metropolitan area") had an estimated population of 7,106,379.[[16]](#cite_note-19) Toronto is an international centre of business, finance, arts, sports, and culture, and is recognized as one of the most [multicultural](/wiki/Multiculturalism "Multiculturalism") and [cosmopolitan](/wiki/Cosmopolitanism "Cosmopolitanism") cities in the world.[[17]](#cite_note-Vipond2017-20)[[18]](#cite_note-HuskenNeubert2011-21)[[19]](#cite_note-22)

[Indigenous peoples](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_in_Canada "Indigenous peoples in Canada") have inhabited the Toronto area, located on a broad sloping [plateau](/wiki/Plateau "Plateau") interspersed with [rivers, deep ravines, and urban forest](/wiki/Toronto_ravine_system "Toronto ravine system"), for more than 10,000 years.[[20]](#cite_note-23) After the broadly disputed [Toronto Purchase](/wiki/Toronto_Purchase "Toronto Purchase"), when the [Mississaugas](/wiki/Mississaugas "Mississaugas") surrendered the area to the [British Crown](/wiki/The_Crown "The Crown"),[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJohnsonWilson198934-24) the British established the town of [York](/wiki/York,_Upper_Canada "York, Upper Canada") in 1793 and later designated it as the capital of [Upper Canada](/wiki/Upper_Canada "Upper Canada").[[22]](#cite_note-25) During the [War of 1812](/wiki/War_of_1812 "War of 1812") the town was the site of the [Battle of York](/wiki/Battle_of_York "Battle of York"), resulting in heavy damage and a two-week [occupation](/wiki/Military_occupation "Military occupation") by [American troops](/wiki/United_States_Armed_Forces "United States Armed Forces").[[23]](#cite_note-26) York was [renamed](/wiki/Name_of_Toronto "Name of Toronto") and incorporated in 1834 as the City of Toronto. It was designated as the capital of the province of Ontario in 1867 during [Canadian Confederation](/wiki/Canadian_Confederation "Canadian Confederation").[[24]](#cite_note-27) Toronto's [city proper](/wiki/City_proper "City proper") has since expanded past its original limits through both [annexation and amalgamation](/wiki/Amalgamation_of_Toronto "Amalgamation of Toronto") to its current area of 631 square kilometres (244 square miles) in 1998.

The diverse population of Toronto reflects its current and historical role as an important destination for [immigrants to Canada](/wiki/Immigration_to_Canada "Immigration to Canada").[[25]](#cite_note-toronto_diversity-28)[[26]](#cite_note-diverse_city-29) About half of its residents were born outside of Canada and over 200 [ethnic origins](/wiki/Ethnic_group "Ethnic group") are represented among its inhabitants.[[27]](#cite_note-Diversity_Toronto_Facts-30) While the majority of Torontonians speak English as their primary language, over 160 languages are spoken in the city.[[28]](#cite_note-2011_census:_Language-31) The city is governed by [Toronto City Council](/wiki/Toronto_City_Council "Toronto City Council"), a [unicameral](/wiki/Unicameralism "Unicameralism") body whose members are elected every four years. City council is composed of 25 councillors, who each represent a geographical ward, and the [mayor of Toronto](/wiki/Mayor_of_Toronto "Mayor of Toronto") who serves as head of council and the [chief executive officer](/wiki/Chief_executive_officer "Chief executive officer") of the [municipal government](/wiki/Municipal_government_of_Toronto "Municipal government of Toronto").[[29]](#cite_note-:4-32)[[30]](#cite_note-Council_Members-33)

Toronto is Canada's largest financial centre, and is home to the [Toronto Stock Exchange](/wiki/Toronto_Stock_Exchange "Toronto Stock Exchange"), the headquarters of Canada's [five largest banks](/wiki/Big_Five_banks_of_Canada "Big Five banks of Canada"),[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDuffy2004154-34) and the headquarters of many large Canadian and multinational corporations.[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDinnie201121-35) [Its economy](/wiki/Economy_of_Toronto "Economy of Toronto") is highly diversified with strengths in technology, design, financial services, life sciences, education, arts, fashion, aerospace, environmental innovation, food services, and tourism.[[33]](#cite_note-36)[[34]](#cite_note-37)[[35]](#cite_note-38) Toronto is a prominent centre for music,[[36]](#cite_note-Toronto_Music-39) theatre,[[37]](#cite_note-Toronto_Theatre-40) motion picture production,[[38]](#cite_note-Toronto_motion_picture_production-41) television production,[[39]](#cite_note-Toronto_television_production-42) and digital media production,[[40]](#cite_note-Digital_Media_Production_UNESCO-43) and is home to the headquarters of Canada's major national [broadcast networks and media outlets](/wiki/Media_in_Toronto "Media in Toronto").[[41]](#cite_note-Toronto_Entertainment_and_Creative_Cluster-44) Its varied [cultural institutions](/wiki/Culture_in_Toronto "Culture in Toronto"),[[42]](#cite_note-Toronto_Culture_and_Creativity-45) which include numerous [museums and galleries](/wiki/List_of_museums_in_Toronto "List of museums in Toronto"), [festivals and public events](/wiki/Annual_events_in_Toronto "Annual events in Toronto"), entertainment districts, [national historic sites](/wiki/List_of_National_Historic_Sites_of_Canada_in_Toronto "List of National Historic Sites of Canada in Toronto"), and [sports activities](/wiki/Sports_in_Toronto "Sports in Toronto"),[[43]](#cite_note-46) attract over [26 million visitors](/wiki/Tourism_in_Toronto "Tourism in Toronto") each year.[[44]](#cite_note-47)[[45]](#cite_note-48)

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

Main article: [Name of Toronto](/wiki/Name_of_Toronto "Name of Toronto")

The name *Toronto* has been recorded with various spellings in French and English, including *Tarento*, *Tarontha*, *Taronto*, *Toranto*, *Torento*, *Toronto*, and *Toronton*.[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGuillet196949-49) The most frequent early spelling, *Taronto*, referred to "The Narrows", a channel of water through which [Lake Simcoe](/wiki/Lake_Simcoe "Lake Simcoe") discharges into [Lake Couchiching](/wiki/Lake_Couchiching "Lake Couchiching") where the Huron had planted tree saplings to corral fish at what is now the [Mnjikaning Fish Weirs](/wiki/Mnjikaning_Fish_Weirs "Mnjikaning Fish Weirs") site in [Orillia](/wiki/Orillia "Orillia"). This narrows was called "*tkaronto*" by the [Mohawk](/wiki/Mohawk_people "Mohawk people"), meaning "where there are trees standing in the water",[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENatural_Resources_Canada-50)[[48]](#cite_note-etymology-51)[[49]](#cite_note-52) and was recorded as early as 1615 by [Samuel de Champlain](/wiki/Samuel_de_Champlain "Samuel de Champlain").[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENatural_Resources_Canada:_Canada,_Provinces_&_Territories:_The_naming_of_their_capital_cities-53) The word *Toronto*, meaning "plenty", also appears in a 1632 French lexicon of the [Huron language](/wiki/Wyandot_language "Wyandot language"), which is also an Iroquoian language.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHounsom197026-54) *Toronto* also appears on French maps referring to various locations, including Georgian Bay, Lake Simcoe, and several rivers.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHounsom197027-55) A [portage](/wiki/Portage "Portage") route from Lake Ontario to [Lake Huron](/wiki/Lake_Huron "Lake Huron") running through this point, known as the "[Toronto Carrying-Place Trail](/wiki/Toronto_Carrying-Place_Trail "Toronto Carrying-Place Trail")", led to widespread use of the name.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Toronto](/wiki/History_of_Toronto "History of Toronto")

For a chronological guide, see [Timeline of Toronto history](/wiki/Timeline_of_Toronto_history "Timeline of Toronto history").

### Early history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early history")]

Archaeological sites show evidence of human occupation in the site that would later become "Toronto" dating back thousands of years.[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFirth19623-56) The [Wendat](/wiki/Wendat "Wendat"), an Iroquoian speaking agricultural people, occupied the territory that would become Toronto for centuries from pre-European contact until the invasion and massacre by the [Haudenosaunee](/wiki/Haudenosaunee "Haudenosaunee"), another Iroquoian speaking agricultural people, from the south side of Lake Ontario between 1648 and 1650. By the 1660s, the [Haudenosaunee established two villages](/wiki/Iroquois_settlement_of_the_north_shore_of_Lake_Ontario "Iroquois settlement of the north shore of Lake Ontario") within what is today Toronto, [Ganatsekwyagon](/wiki/Bead_Hill "Bead Hill") (Bead Hill) on the banks of the [Rouge River](/wiki/Rouge_River_(Ontario) "Rouge River (Ontario)") and [Teiaiagon](/wiki/Teiaiagon "Teiaiagon") on the banks of the [Humber River](/wiki/Humber_River_(Ontario) "Humber River (Ontario)"). By 1701, the [Mississaugas](/wiki/Mississaugas "Mississaugas"), an [Anishinaabe](/wiki/Anishinaabe "Anishinaabe") speaking [hunter-gatherer](/wiki/Hunter-gatherer "Hunter-gatherer") people from [Northern Ontario](/wiki/Northern_Ontario "Northern Ontario"), had displaced the Haudenosaunee, who abandoned the Toronto area at the end of the [Beaver Wars](/wiki/Beaver_Wars "Beaver Wars"), with most returning to their homeland in present-day New York state.[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchmalz1991-57)

French traders founded [Fort Rouillé](/wiki/Fort_Rouill%C3%A9 "Fort Rouillé") in 1750 (the current [Exhibition grounds](/wiki/Exhibition_Place "Exhibition Place") were later developed there), but abandoned it in 1759 during the [Seven Years' War](/wiki/Seven_Years%27_War "Seven Years' War").[[55]](#cite_note-rouille-58) The British defeated the French and their indigenous allies in the war, and the area became part of the British colony of [Quebec](/wiki/Province_of_Quebec_(1763%E2%80%931791) "Province of Quebec (1763–1791)") in 1763.

During the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"), an influx of British settlers arrived there as [United Empire Loyalists](/wiki/United_Empire_Loyalist "United Empire Loyalist") fled for the British-controlled lands north of Lake Ontario. The Crown granted them land to compensate for their losses in the Thirteen Colonies. The new province of Upper Canada was being created and needed a capital. In 1787, the British [Lord Dorchester](/wiki/Guy_Carleton,_1st_Baron_Dorchester "Guy Carleton, 1st Baron Dorchester") arranged for the Toronto Purchase with the Mississaugas of the New Credit First Nation, thereby securing more than a quarter of a million acres (1000 km2) of land in the Toronto area.[[56]](#cite_note-British-59) Dorchester intended the location to be named Toronto.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHounsom197027-55) The first 25 years after the Toronto purchase were quiet, although "there were occasional independent fur traders" present in the area, with the usual complaints of debauchery and drunkenness.[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFirth19623-56)

### Town of York (1793–1834)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Town of York (1793–1834)")]

In 1793, Governor [John Graves Simcoe](/wiki/John_Graves_Simcoe "John Graves Simcoe") established the town of [York](/wiki/York,_Upper_Canada "York, Upper Canada") on the Toronto Purchase lands, naming it after [Prince Frederick, Duke of York and Albany](/wiki/Prince_Frederick,_Duke_of_York_and_Albany "Prince Frederick, Duke of York and Albany"). Simcoe decided to move the Upper Canada capital from [Newark](/wiki/Niagara-on-the-Lake "Niagara-on-the-Lake") (Niagara-on-the-Lake) to York,[[57]](#cite_note-60) believing the new site would be less vulnerable to attack by the United States.[[58]](#cite_note-[[Fort_York]]-61) The [York garrison](/wiki/Fort_York "Fort York") was built at the entrance of the town's natural harbour, sheltered by a long sand-bar peninsula. The town's settlement formed at the harbour's eastern end behind the peninsula, near the present-day intersection of [Parliament Street](/wiki/Parliament_Street_(Toronto) "Parliament Street (Toronto)") and [Front Street](/wiki/Front_Street_(Toronto) "Front Street (Toronto)") (in the "[Old Town](/wiki/Old_Town,_Toronto "Old Town, Toronto")" area).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/Battle_of_York_airborne.jpg/250px-Battle_of_York_airborne.jpg)](/wiki/File:Battle_of_York_airborne.jpg)

An American squadron exchanging fire with [Fort York](/wiki/Fort_York "Fort York") during the [Battle of York](/wiki/Battle_of_York "Battle of York"), 1813. The American landing is depicted to the west (left foreground).

In 1813, as part of the [War of 1812](/wiki/War_of_1812 "War of 1812"), the [Battle of York](/wiki/Battle_of_York "Battle of York") ended in the town's capture and plunder by United States forces.[[59]](#cite_note-62) [John Strachan](/wiki/John_Strachan "John Strachan") negotiated the town's surrender. American soldiers destroyed much of the garrison and set fire to the parliament buildings during their five-day occupation. Because of the sacking of York, British troops retaliated later in the war with the [burning of Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Burning_of_Washington "Burning of Washington")

The [University of Toronto](/wiki/University_of_Toronto "University of Toronto"), then known as King's College, was established in 1827 as the first institution of higher education in Upper Canada.[[60]](#cite_note-63)

### Incorporation and development (1834–1954)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Incorporation and development (1834–1954)")]

York was incorporated as the "City of Toronto" on March 6, 1834, adopting the Indigenous name, *Toronto*.[[61]](#cite_note-64) Reformist politician [William Lyon Mackenzie](/wiki/William_Lyon_Mackenzie "William Lyon Mackenzie") became the first [mayor of Toronto](/wiki/Mayor_of_Toronto "Mayor of Toronto"). Mackenzie would later lead the unsuccessful [Upper Canada Rebellion](/wiki/Upper_Canada_Rebellion "Upper Canada Rebellion") of 1837 against the British colonial government.

Toronto's population of 9,000 included some [African-American](/wiki/African_Americans "African Americans") slaves,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] some of whom had been brought by the Loyalists, and [Black Loyalists](/wiki/Black_Loyalist "Black Loyalist"), whom the Crown had freed (most of the latter were resettled in Nova Scotia). By 1834, refugee slaves from America's South were also immigrating to Toronto to gain freedom.[[62]](#cite_note-black-65) [Slavery](/wiki/Slavery_in_Canada "Slavery in Canada") was banned outright in Upper Canada (and throughout the British Empire) in 1834.[[63]](#cite_note-66) Torontonians integrated people of colour into their society. In the 1840s, an eating house at Frederick and King Streets, a place of mercantile prosperity in the early city, was operated by a black man named Bloxom.[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERobertson189425-67)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/TorontoCanadaWest.jpg/250px-TorontoCanadaWest.jpg)](/wiki/File:TorontoCanadaWest.jpg)

Toronto in 1854. The city was a major destination for immigrants to Canada in the second half of the 19th century.

As a major destination for immigrants to Canada, the city grew rapidly through the remainder of the 19th century. The first significant wave of immigrants were Irish, fleeing the [Great Irish Famine](/wiki/Great_Famine_(Ireland) "Great Famine (Ireland)"); most of them were [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"). By 1851, the Irish-born population had become the largest single ethnic group in the city. The Scottish and English population welcomed smaller numbers of [Protestant](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") Irish immigrants, some from what is now Northern Ireland, which gave the [Orange Order](/wiki/Orange_Order "Orange Order") significant and long-lasting influence over Toronto society. Almost every mayor of Toronto was a member of the Orange Order between 1850 and 1950, and the city was sometimes referred to as the "[Belfast](/wiki/Belfast "Belfast") of Canada" because of Orange influence in municipal politics and administration.[[65]](#cite_note-68)

For brief periods, Toronto was twice the capital of the united [Province of Canada](/wiki/Province_of_Canada "Province of Canada"): first from 1849 to 1851, following unrest in Montreal, and later from 1855 to 1859. After this date, Quebec was designated as the capital until 1865 (two years before Canadian Confederation). Since then, the capital of Canada has remained as [Ottawa](/wiki/Ottawa "Ottawa"), Ontario.[[66]](#cite_note-69)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/ParliamentBuildingsFrontStreet.jpg/250px-ParliamentBuildingsFrontStreet.jpg)](/wiki/File:ParliamentBuildingsFrontStreet.jpg)

The second Parliament of Upper Canada building on Front Street, 1856

Toronto became the capital of the province of Ontario after its official creation in 1867. The seat of government of Ontario briefly returned to the same building that had served as the Third Parliament Building of Upper Canada, before moving to the [Ontario Legislative Building](/wiki/Ontario_Legislative_Building "Ontario Legislative Building") at [Queen's Park](/wiki/Queen%27s_Park_(Toronto) "Queen's Park (Toronto)") in 1893. Because of its provincial capital status, the city was also the location of [Government House](/wiki/Government_House_(Ontario) "Government House (Ontario)"), the residence of the [viceregal](/wiki/Viceroy "Viceroy") representative of [the Crown](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Canada "Monarchy of Canada") [in right of Ontario](/wiki/Monarchy_in_Ontario "Monarchy in Ontario").

Long before the [Royal Military College of Canada](/wiki/Royal_Military_College_of_Canada "Royal Military College of Canada") was established in 1876, supporters of the concept proposed military colleges in Canada. Staffed by British Regulars, adult male students underwent a three-month-long military course at the School of Military Instruction in Toronto. Established by Militia General Order in 1864, the school enabled officers of militia or candidates for commission or promotion in the Militia to learn military duties, drill and discipline, to command a company at Battalion Drill, to drill a company at Company Drill, the internal economy of a company, and the duties of a company's officer.[[67]](#cite_note-70) The school was retained at Confederation, in 1867. In 1868, schools of [cavalry](/wiki/Cavalry "Cavalry") and [artillery](/wiki/Artillery "Artillery") instruction were formed in Toronto.[[68]](#cite_note-71)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Yorkville_Town_Hall%2C_Yonge_St.%2C_west_side%2C_opposite_Collier_St._%2816526085614%29.jpg/250px-Yorkville_Town_Hall%2C_Yonge_St.%2C_west_side%2C_opposite_Collier_St._%2816526085614%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Yorkville_Town_Hall,_Yonge_St.,_west_side,_opposite_Collier_St._(16526085614).jpg)

A group in front of a horse-drawn streetcar in front of [Yorkville Town Hall](/wiki/Yorkville_Town_Hall "Yorkville Town Hall") 1870. A [gas streetlamp](/wiki/Gas_lighting "Gas lighting") is visible in the right foreground.

In the 19th century, the city built an extensive sewage system to improve sanitation, and streets were illuminated with [gas lighting](/wiki/Gas_lighting "Gas lighting") as a regular service.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Long-distance railway lines were constructed, including a route completed in 1854 linking Toronto with the Upper Great Lakes. The [Grand Trunk Railway](/wiki/Grand_Trunk_Railway "Grand Trunk Railway") and the [Northern Railway of Canada](/wiki/Northern_Railway_of_Canada "Northern Railway of Canada") joined in the building of the first [Union Station](/wiki/Toronto_Union_Station_(1858) "Toronto Union Station (1858)") downtown. The advent of the railway dramatically increased the numbers of immigrants arriving, commerce and industry, as had the Lake Ontario steamers and schooners entering port before. These enabled Toronto to become a major gateway linking the world to the interior of the North American continent. Expanding port and rail facilities brought in northern timber for export and imported Pennsylvania coal. Industry dominated the waterfront for the next 100 years.

During the late 19th century, Toronto became the largest alcohol distillation (in particular, [spirits](/wiki/Distilled_beverage "Distilled beverage")) centre in North America.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] A distillery built by [Gooderham and Worts](/wiki/Gooderham_and_Worts "Gooderham and Worts") from 1959 to 1961 became the country's largest [whisky](/wiki/Whisky "Whisky") factory.[[69]](#cite_note-72)[[70]](#cite_note-Kearney2002-73) While the factory has since closed, its buildings have been designated a [National Historic Site](/wiki/National_Historic_Sites_of_Canada "National Historic Sites of Canada") and have been converted into the [Distillery District](/wiki/Distillery_District "Distillery District").[[71]](#cite_note-74)[[72]](#cite_note-75) The harbour allowed access to grain and sugar imports used in processing.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/GooderhamAndWorts1800s.jpg/250px-GooderhamAndWorts1800s.jpg)](/wiki/File:GooderhamAndWorts1800s.jpg)

The [Gooderham and Worts](/wiki/Gooderham_and_Worts "Gooderham and Worts") buildings, 1896

Horse-drawn [streetcars](/wiki/Tram "Tram") gave way to electric streetcars in 1891 when the city granted the operation of the transit franchise to the [Toronto Railway Company](/wiki/Toronto_Railway_Company "Toronto Railway Company"). The public transit system passed into public ownership in 1921 as the [Toronto Transportation Commission](/wiki/Toronto_Transportation_Commission "Toronto Transportation Commission"), later renamed the [Toronto Transit Commission](/wiki/Toronto_Transit_Commission "Toronto Transit Commission"). The system now has [the third-highest ridership of any city public transportation system in North America](/wiki/List_of_North_American_rapid_transit_systems_by_ridership "List of North American rapid transit systems by ridership").[[73]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-76)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/Toronto_Fire_Ruins%2C_Front_Street_April_19th%2C_1904_%28HS85-10-14899%29.jpg/250px-Toronto_Fire_Ruins%2C_Front_Street_April_19th%2C_1904_%28HS85-10-14899%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Toronto_Fire_Ruins,_Front_Street_April_19th,_1904_(HS85-10-14899).jpg)

Ruins on Front Street after the [Great Toronto Fire of 1904](/wiki/Great_Fire_of_Toronto_(1904) "Great Fire of Toronto (1904)")

The [Great Toronto Fire of 1904](/wiki/Great_Fire_of_Toronto_(1904) "Great Fire of Toronto (1904)") destroyed a large section of [downtown Toronto](/wiki/Downtown_Toronto "Downtown Toronto"). The fire destroyed more than 100 buildings.[[74]](#cite_note-Morris1992-77) The fire claimed one victim, John Croft, who was an explosive expert clearing the ruins from the fire.[[75]](#cite_note-Filey1999-78) It caused CA$10,387,000 (equivalent to $390,356,450 in 2025) in damage.[[76]](#cite_note-Evening_Citizen_1948-79)

The city received new European immigrant groups from the late 19th century into the early 20th century, particularly Germans, French, Italians, and Jews. They were soon followed by Russians, Poles, and other Eastern European nations, in addition to the Chinese entering from the West. Like the Irish before them, many of these migrants lived in overcrowded shanty-type slums, such as "[the Ward](/wiki/The_Ward,_Toronto "The Ward, Toronto")", which was centred on [Bay Street](/wiki/Bay_Street "Bay Street"), now the heart of the country's [Financial District](/wiki/Financial_District,_Toronto "Financial District, Toronto").

As new migrants began to prosper, they moved to better housing in other areas, in what is now understood to be succession waves of settlement. Despite its fast-paced growth, by the 1920s, Toronto's population and economic importance in Canada remained second to the much longer established [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal"), Quebec. However, by 1934, the Toronto Stock Exchange had become the largest in the country.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/VE_Day_celebrations_on_Bay_Street_1945.jpg/250px-VE_Day_celebrations_on_Bay_Street_1945.jpg)](/wiki/File:VE_Day_celebrations_on_Bay_Street_1945.jpg)

People in Toronto celebrating the [Victory in Europe](/wiki/Victory_in_Europe_Day "Victory in Europe Day"), May 1945

### Metro era (1954–1997)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Metro era (1954–1997)")]

In 1954, the City of Toronto and 12 surrounding municipalities were federated into a [regional government](/wiki/Regional_municipality "Regional municipality") known as [Metropolitan Toronto](/wiki/Metropolitan_Toronto "Metropolitan Toronto").[[77]](#cite_note-80) The postwar boom had resulted in rapid suburban development. It was believed a coordinated land-use strategy and shared services would provide greater efficiency for the region. The metropolitan government began to manage services that crossed municipal boundaries, including highways, police services, water and [public transit](/wiki/Public_transport "Public transport"). In that year, a half-century after the Great Fire of 1904, disaster struck the city again when [Hurricane Hazel](/wiki/Hurricane_Hazel "Hurricane Hazel") brought intense winds and flash flooding. In the Toronto area, 81 people were killed, nearly 1,900 families were left homeless, and the hurricane caused more than CA$25,000,000 (equivalent to $291,134,752 in 2025) in damage.[[78]](#cite_note-hurricane-81)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/Hurricane_Hazel_--_house2.jpg/250px-Hurricane_Hazel_--_house2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hurricane_Hazel_--_house2.jpg)

Houses uprooted by flooding of the Humber River after [Hurricane Hazel](/wiki/Hurricane_Hazel "Hurricane Hazel") passed through Toronto, 1954

In 1967, the seven smallest municipalities of Metropolitan Toronto were merged with larger neighbours, resulting in a six-municipality configuration that included the [former city of Toronto](/wiki/Old_Toronto "Old Toronto") and the surrounding municipalities of [East York](/wiki/East_York "East York"), [Etobicoke](/wiki/Etobicoke "Etobicoke"), [North York](/wiki/North_York "North York"), [Scarborough](/wiki/Scarborough,_Ontario "Scarborough, Ontario"), and [York](/wiki/York,_Ontario "York, Ontario").[[79]](#cite_note-82)

In the decades after World War II, refugees from war-torn Europe and Chinese job-seekers arrived, as well as construction labourers, particularly from Italy and Portugal. Toronto's population grew to more than one million in 1951 when large-scale suburbanization began and doubled to two million by 1971. Following the elimination of racially based immigration policies by the late 1960s, Toronto became a destination for immigrants from all over the world. By the 1980s, Toronto had surpassed Montreal as Canada's most populous city and chief economic hub. During this time, in part owing to the political uncertainty raised by the resurgence of the [Quebec sovereignty movement](/wiki/Quebec_sovereignty_movement "Quebec sovereignty movement"), many national and multinational corporations moved their head offices from Montreal to Toronto and [Western Canadian](/wiki/Western_Canada "Western Canada") cities.[[80]](#cite_note-83)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/-First_Canadian_Place_under_construction-3.jpg/250px--First_Canadian_Place_under_construction-3.jpg)](/wiki/File:-First_Canadian_Place_under_construction-3.jpg)

Construction of [First Canadian Place](/wiki/First_Canadian_Place "First Canadian Place"), the operational headquarters of the [Bank of Montreal](/wiki/Bank_of_Montreal "Bank of Montreal"), in 1975

### "Megacity" era (1998–present)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: \"Megacity\" era (1998–present)")]

On January 1, 1998, Toronto was greatly enlarged, not through traditional [annexations](/wiki/Annexation "Annexation"), but as an [amalgamation](/wiki/Merger_(politics) "Merger (politics)") of the Municipality of Metropolitan Toronto and its six lower-tier constituent municipalities: East York, Etobicoke, North York, Scarborough, York, and the original city itself. They were dissolved by an act of the [Government of Ontario](/wiki/Government_of_Ontario "Government of Ontario") and formed into a single-tier City of Toronto (colloquially dubbed the "[megacity](/wiki/Merger_(politics)#Canada "Merger (politics)")"), replacing all six governments.

The merger was proposed as a cost-saving measure by the [Progressive Conservative](/wiki/Progressive_Conservative_Party_of_Ontario "Progressive Conservative Party of Ontario") provincial government under premier [Mike Harris](/wiki/Mike_Harris "Mike Harris"). The announcement touched off vociferous public objections. In March 1997, a [referendum](/wiki/Referendum "Referendum") in all six municipalities produced a vote of more than 3:1 against amalgamation.[[81]](#cite_note-84) However, municipal governments in Canada are creatures of the provincial governments, and referendums have little to no legal effect. The Harris government could thus legally ignore the referendum results and did so in April when it tabled the *[City of Toronto Act](/wiki/City_of_Toronto_Act "City of Toronto Act")*. Both [opposition parties](/wiki/Parliamentary_opposition "Parliamentary opposition") [held a filibuster](/wiki/Filibuster#Provincial "Filibuster") in the provincial legislature, proposing more than 12,000 amendments that allowed residents on streets of the proposed megacity to take part in public hearings on the merger and adding historical designations to the streets.[[82]](#cite_note-85) This only delayed the bill's inevitable passage, given the Progressive Conservatives' majority.

North York mayor [Mel Lastman](/wiki/Mel_Lastman "Mel Lastman") became the first "megacity" mayor, and the 62nd mayor of Toronto, with his [electoral victory](/wiki/1997_Toronto_municipal_election "1997 Toronto municipal election").[[83]](#cite_note-1997_results-86) Lastman gained national attention after multiple snowstorms, including the January [Blizzard of 1999](/wiki/North_American_blizzard_of_1999 "North American blizzard of 1999"), dumped 118 centimetres (46 in) of snow and effectively immobilized the city.[[84]](#cite_note-Mel_calls_in_the_troops-87)[[85]](#cite_note-88) He called in the [Canadian Army](/wiki/Canadian_Army "Canadian Army") to aid snow removal by use of their equipment to augment police and emergency services. The move was ridiculed by some in other parts of the country, fuelled in part by what was perceived as a frivolous use of resources.[[86]](#cite_note-89)[[87]](#cite_note-Megacity_Mel_Snowstorm_critique-90)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/Jasonp_blackout2003_toronto_UnionStnInt.jpg/250px-Jasonp_blackout2003_toronto_UnionStnInt.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jasonp_blackout2003_toronto_UnionStnInt.jpg)

Crowds navigating [Union Station](/wiki/Union_Station_(Toronto) "Union Station (Toronto)") during the [Northeast blackout of 2003](/wiki/Northeast_blackout_of_2003 "Northeast blackout of 2003")

The city attracted international attention in 2003 when it became the centre of a major [SARS](/wiki/SARS "SARS") (severe acute respiratory syndrome) outbreak. Public health attempts to prevent the disease from spreading elsewhere temporarily dampened the local economy.[[88]](#cite_note-91) From August 14 to 17, 2003, the city was hit by a [massive blackout](/wiki/Northeast_blackout_of_2003 "Northeast blackout of 2003") which affected millions of Torontonians (it also affected most of Southern Ontario and parts of the United States), stranding some hundreds of people in tall buildings, knocking out traffic lights and suspending subway and streetcar service across the city during those aforementioned days.[[89]](#cite_note-92)

On March 6, 2009, the city celebrated the 175th anniversary of its inception as the City of Toronto in 1834. Toronto hosted the [4th G20 summit](/wiki/2010_G20_Toronto_summit "2010 G20 Toronto summit") during June 26–27, 2010. This included the largest security operation in Canadian history. Following large-scale [protests](/wiki/2010_G20_Toronto_summit_protests "2010 G20 Toronto summit protests") and rioting, law enforcement arrested more than 1,000 people, the largest mass arrest in Canadian history.[[90]](#cite_note-93)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/Tree_falls_on_vehicle_-_Toronto_Ice_Storm_2013.jpg/250px-Tree_falls_on_vehicle_-_Toronto_Ice_Storm_2013.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tree_falls_on_vehicle_-_Toronto_Ice_Storm_2013.jpg)

Damage from a fallen tree after the [December 2013 storm complex](/wiki/December_2013_North_American_storm_complex "December 2013 North American storm complex") passed through Toronto

On July 8, 2013, severe flash flooding hit Toronto after an afternoon of slow-moving, intense thunderstorms. Toronto Hydro estimated 450,000 people were without power after the storm and Toronto Pearson International Airport reported 126 millimetres (5.0 in) of rain had fallen over five hours, more than during Hurricane Hazel.[[91]](#cite_note-Thunderstorm_2013-94) Within six months, from December 20 to 22, 2013, Toronto was brought to a near halt by the worst [ice storm](/wiki/December_2013_North_American_storm_complex "December 2013 North American storm complex") in the city's history, rivalling the severity of the [1998 Ice Storm](/wiki/January_1998_North_American_ice_storm "January 1998 North American ice storm") (which mainly affected southeastern Ontario, and Quebec). At the height of the storm, over 300,000 [Toronto Hydro](/wiki/Toronto_Hydro "Toronto Hydro") customers had no electricity or heating.[[92]](#cite_note-95) Toronto hosted [WorldPride](/wiki/WorldPride "WorldPride") in June 2014,[[93]](#cite_note-96) and the [Pan](/wiki/Pan_American_Games "Pan American Games") and [Parapan American Games](/wiki/Parapan_American_Games "Parapan American Games") in [2015](/wiki/2015_Pan_American_Games "2015 Pan American Games").[[94]](#cite_note-97)

In January 2020, the COVID-19 first arrived in Canada in Toronto and [the resulting pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Toronto "COVID-19 pandemic in Toronto") killed 4,940 people in the city within four years.[[95]](#cite_note-98)[[96]](#cite_note-RC-99)[[97]](#cite_note-100)

In the 2010s, the city continued to grow and attract immigrants. A 2019 study by [Toronto Metropolitan University](/wiki/Toronto_Metropolitan_University "Toronto Metropolitan University") (then known as Ryerson University) showed that Toronto was the fastest-growing city in North America. The city added 77,435 people between July 2017 and July 2018. The large growth in the Toronto metropolitan area was attributed to international migration to Toronto.[[98]](#cite_note-101) After a drop around the beginning of the COVID-19 pandemic, Toronto's population surged, despite extinction-level [fertility rates](/wiki/Fertility_rate "Fertility rate"), to an estimated 3.28 million in 2024, a 17% increase from the 2021 census population.[[7]](#cite_note-statscanestimates-8) During this time, Greater Toronto Area was the fastest-growing metropolitan area in [Northern America](/wiki/Northern_America "Northern America"), and this growth was "driven by Canada's high levels of international immigration in recent years".[[99]](#cite_note-102) In 2025, the city's population began dropping as Canada experienced its largest population decrease in history, mostly due to the [Carney](/wiki/Mark_Carney "Mark Carney") administration's restrictions on international students and immigration.[[7]](#cite_note-statscanestimates-8)[[100]](#cite_note-103)[[101]](#cite_note-104)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Geography")]

Main article: [Geography of Toronto](/wiki/Geography_of_Toronto "Geography of Toronto")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Toronto_by_Sentinel-2.jpg/250px-Toronto_by_Sentinel-2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Toronto_by_Sentinel-2.jpg)

Satellite image of Toronto and the surrounding suburbs in 2018

Toronto covers an area of 631 square kilometres (244 sq mi),[[4]](#cite_note-CityPop-5) with a maximum north–south distance of 21 kilometres (13 mi). It has a maximum east–west distance of 43 kilometres (27 mi), and it has a 46-kilometre (29 mi) long [waterfront](/wiki/Toronto_waterfront "Toronto waterfront") shoreline, on the northwestern shore of [Lake Ontario](/wiki/Lake_Ontario "Lake Ontario"). The [Toronto Islands](/wiki/Toronto_Islands "Toronto Islands") and [Port Lands](/wiki/Port_Lands "Port Lands") extend out into the lake, allowing for a somewhat sheltered [Toronto Harbour](/wiki/Toronto_Harbour "Toronto Harbour") south of the downtown core.[[102]](#cite_note-northwest-105) An Outer Harbour was constructed southeast of downtown during the 1950s and 1960s, and it is now used for recreation. Toronto's limits are formed by Lake Ontario to the south, the western boundary of [Marie Curtis Park](/wiki/Marie_Curtis_Park "Marie Curtis Park"), [Etobicoke Creek](/wiki/Etobicoke_Creek "Etobicoke Creek"), [Eglinton Avenue](/wiki/Eglinton_Avenue "Eglinton Avenue"), and [Highway 427](/wiki/Ontario_Highway_427 "Ontario Highway 427") to the west, [Steeles Avenue](/wiki/Steeles_Avenue "Steeles Avenue") to the north, and the [Rouge River](/wiki/Rouge_River_(Ontario) "Rouge River (Ontario)") and the Scarborough–Pickering Townline to the east.

### Topography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Topography")]

Further information: [Toronto ravine system](/wiki/Toronto_ravine_system "Toronto ravine system")

|  |  |
| --- | --- |

Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *(July 2016)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Leaside_Bridge_2023.jpg/250px-Leaside_Bridge_2023.jpg)](/wiki/File:Leaside_Bridge_2023.jpg)

[Leaside Bridge](/wiki/Leaside_Bridge "Leaside Bridge") crossing the [Don valley ravine](/wiki/Don_River_(Ontario) "Don River (Ontario)") in 2023. The [Toronto ravine system](/wiki/Toronto_ravine_system "Toronto ravine system") and its [waterways](/wiki/Toronto_waterway_system "Toronto waterway system") cut through the city's landscape.

The city is mostly flat or gentle hills, and the land gently slopes upward away from the lake.[[103]](#cite_note-106) The flat land is interrupted by the [Toronto ravine system](/wiki/Toronto_ravine_system "Toronto ravine system"), which is cut by numerous creeks and rivers of the [Toronto waterway system](/wiki/Toronto_waterway_system "Toronto waterway system"), most notably the Humber River in the west end, the [Don River](/wiki/Don_River_(Ontario) "Don River (Ontario)") east of downtown (these two rivers flanking and defining the Toronto Harbour), and the Rouge River at the city's eastern limits. Most of the ravines and valley lands in Toronto today are parklands and recreational trails are laid out along the ravines and valleys.[[104]](#cite_note-107) The original town was laid out in a [grid plan](/wiki/Grid_plan "Grid plan") on the flat plain north of the harbour, and this plan was extended outwards as the city grew. The width and depth of several of the ravines and valleys are such that several grid streets, such as [Finch Avenue](/wiki/Finch_Avenue "Finch Avenue"), [Leslie Street](/wiki/Leslie_Street "Leslie Street"), [Lawrence Avenue](/wiki/Lawrence_Avenue "Lawrence Avenue"), and [St. Clair Avenue](/wiki/St._Clair_Avenue "St. Clair Avenue"), terminate on one side of a ravine or valley and continue on the other side. Toronto has many bridges spanning the ravines. Large bridges such as the [Prince Edward Viaduct](/wiki/Prince_Edward_Viaduct "Prince Edward Viaduct") were built to span broad river valleys.

Despite its deep ravines, Toronto is not remarkably hilly, but its elevation does increase steadily away from the lake. Elevation differences range from 76.5 metres (251 feet) [above sea level](/wiki/Above_mean_sea_level "Above mean sea level") at the Lake Ontario shore to 209 m (686 ft) above sea level near the [York University](/wiki/York_University "York University") grounds in the city's north end at the intersection of [Keele Street](/wiki/Keele_Street "Keele Street") and Steeles Avenue.[[105]](#cite_note-108) There are occasional hilly areas; in particular, [midtown Toronto](/wiki/Midtown,_Toronto "Midtown, Toronto"), as well as the [Silverthorn](/wiki/Silverthorn,_Toronto "Silverthorn, Toronto") and [Fairbank](/wiki/Fairbank,_Toronto "Fairbank, Toronto") neighbourhoods, have several sharply sloping hills. Lake Ontario remains occasionally visible from the peaks of these ridges as far north as Eglinton Avenue, 7 to 8 kilometres (4.3 to 5.0 mi) inland.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/Topographical_Map_of_Toronto.png/330px-Topographical_Map_of_Toronto.png)](/wiki/File:Topographical_Map_of_Toronto.png)

Topographical map of Toronto. The terrain elevation increases steadily away from the shoreline.

The other major geographical feature of Toronto is its escarpments. During the [last ice age](/wiki/Last_Glacial_Period "Last Glacial Period"), the lower part of Toronto was beneath [Glacial Lake Iroquois](/wiki/Glacial_Lake_Iroquois "Glacial Lake Iroquois"). Today, a series of [escarpments](/wiki/Escarpment "Escarpment") mark the lake's former boundary, known as the "Iroquois Shoreline". The escarpments are most prominent from [Victoria Park Avenue](/wiki/Victoria_Park_Avenue "Victoria Park Avenue") to the mouth of [Highland Creek](/wiki/Highland_Creek_(Toronto) "Highland Creek (Toronto)"), where they form the [Scarborough Bluffs](/wiki/Scarborough_Bluffs "Scarborough Bluffs"). Other observable sections include the area near St. Clair Avenue West between [Bathurst Street](/wiki/Bathurst_Street_(Toronto) "Bathurst Street (Toronto)") and the Don River, and north of [Davenport Road](/wiki/Davenport_Road "Davenport Road") from Caledonia to [Spadina Road](/wiki/Spadina_Avenue "Spadina Avenue"); the [Casa Loma](/wiki/Casa_Loma "Casa Loma") grounds sit above this escarpment.[[106]](#cite_note-109)

The geography of the lakeshore has dramatically changed since the first settlement of Toronto. Much of the land on the harbour's north shore is landfill, filled in during the late 19th century. Until then, the lakefront docks (then known as wharves) were set back farther inland than today. Much of the adjacent Port Lands on the harbour's east side was a [wetland](/wiki/Wetland "Wetland") filled in early in the 20th century.[[107]](#cite_note-utoronto1-110) The shoreline from the harbour west to the Humber River has been extended into the lake. Further west, landfill has been used to create extensions of land such as Humber Bay Park.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/Views_from_the_CN_Tower_Laslovarga_%2820%29.JPG/250px-Views_from_the_CN_Tower_Laslovarga_%2820%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:Views_from_the_CN_Tower_Laslovarga_(20).JPG)

View of the [Toronto Islands](/wiki/Toronto_Islands "Toronto Islands"), an island chain that bounds [Toronto's Inner Harbour](/wiki/Toronto_Harbour#Inner_Harbour "Toronto Harbour")

The Toronto Islands were a natural peninsula until a storm in 1858 severed their connection to the mainland,[[108]](#cite_note-111) creating a channel to the harbour. The peninsula was formed by [longshore drift](/wiki/Longshore_drift "Longshore drift") taking the sediments deposited along the Scarborough Bluffs shore and transporting them to the Islands area.

The other source of sediment for the Port Lands wetland and the peninsula was the deposition of the Don River, which carved a wide valley through the sedimentary land of Toronto and deposited it in the shallow harbour. The harbour and the channel of the Don River have been dredged numerous times for shipping. The lower section of the Don River was straightened and channelled in the 19th century. The former mouth drained into a wetland; today, the Don River drains into the harbour through a concrete waterway, the [Keating Channel](/wiki/Keating_Channel "Keating Channel"). To mitigate flooding in the area, as well as to create parkland, a second more natural mouth was built to the south during the first half of the 2020s, thereby creating a new island, [Ookwemin Minising](/wiki/Ookwemin_Minising "Ookwemin Minising").

### Neighbourhoods and former municipalities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Neighbourhoods and former municipalities")]

Main articles: [History of neighbourhoods in Toronto](/wiki/History_of_neighbourhoods_in_Toronto "History of neighbourhoods in Toronto") and [List of neighbourhoods in Toronto](/wiki/List_of_neighbourhoods_in_Toronto "List of neighbourhoods in Toronto")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/Toronto_map.png/250px-Toronto_map.png)](/wiki/File:Toronto_map.png)

Map of Toronto with major traffic routes. Also shown are the limits of [six former municipalities](/wiki/Metropolitan_Toronto "Metropolitan Toronto"), which form the current City of Toronto.

Toronto encompasses an area formerly administered by several separate municipalities that were amalgamated over the years. Each developed a distinct history and identity over the years, and their names remain in common use among Torontonians. Former municipalities include East York, Etobicoke, [Forest Hill](/wiki/Forest_Hill,_Toronto "Forest Hill, Toronto"), [Mimico](/wiki/Mimico "Mimico"), North York, [Parkdale](/wiki/Parkdale,_Toronto "Parkdale, Toronto"), Scarborough, [Swansea](/wiki/Swansea,_Toronto "Swansea, Toronto"), [Weston](/wiki/Weston,_Toronto "Weston, Toronto") and York. Throughout the city, there exist hundreds of small neighbourhoods and some larger neighbourhoods covering a few square kilometres.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The many residential communities of Toronto express a character distinct from the skyscrapers in the commercial core. [Victorian](/wiki/Victorian_era "Victorian era") and [Edwardian-era](/wiki/Edwardian_era "Edwardian era") residential buildings can be found in enclaves such as [Rosedale](/wiki/Rosedale,_Toronto "Rosedale, Toronto"), [Cabbagetown](/wiki/Cabbagetown,_Toronto "Cabbagetown, Toronto"), [The Annex](/wiki/The_Annex "The Annex"), and [Yorkville](/wiki/Yorkville,_Toronto "Yorkville, Toronto").[[109]](#cite_note-112) The [Wychwood Park](/wiki/Wychwood_Park "Wychwood Park") neighbourhood, historically significant for the architecture of its homes, and for being one of Toronto's earliest planned communities, was designated as an Ontario Heritage Conservation district in 1985.[[110]](#cite_note-113) The [Casa Loma](/wiki/Casa_Loma_(neighbourhood) "Casa Loma (neighbourhood)") neighbourhood is named after "Casa Loma", a castle built in 1911 by [Sir Henry Pellat](/wiki/Henry_Pellatt "Henry Pellatt"), complete with gardens, turrets, stables, an elevator, secret passages, and a bowling alley.[[111]](#cite_note-114) [Spadina House](/wiki/Spadina_House "Spadina House") is a 19th-century [manor](/wiki/Manor_house "Manor house") that is now a museum.[[112]](#cite_note-115)

#### Old Toronto

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Old Toronto")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Cabbagetown_houses.jpg/250px-Cabbagetown_houses.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cabbagetown_houses.jpg)

[Victorian](/wiki/Victorian_architecture "Victorian architecture")-era [Bay-and-gable](/wiki/Bay-and-gable "Bay-and-gable") houses are a distinct architectural [style of residence](/wiki/List_of_house_types "List of house types") that is ubiquitous throughout the older neighbourhoods of Toronto.

The pre-amalgamation City of Toronto covers the downtown core and older neighbourhoods to the east, west, and north. It is the most densely populated part of the city. The Financial District contains the [First Canadian Place](/wiki/First_Canadian_Place "First Canadian Place"), [Toronto-Dominion Centre](/wiki/Toronto-Dominion_Centre "Toronto-Dominion Centre"), [Scotia Plaza](/wiki/Scotia_Plaza "Scotia Plaza"), [Royal Bank Plaza](/wiki/Royal_Bank_Plaza "Royal Bank Plaza"), [Commerce Court](/wiki/Commerce_Court "Commerce Court") and [Brookfield Place](/wiki/Brookfield_Place_(Toronto) "Brookfield Place (Toronto)"). This area includes, among others, the neighbourhoods of [St. James Town](/wiki/St._James_Town "St. James Town"), [Garden District](/wiki/Garden_District,_Toronto "Garden District, Toronto"), [St. Lawrence](/wiki/St._Lawrence,_Toronto "St. Lawrence, Toronto"), [Corktown](/wiki/Corktown,_Toronto "Corktown, Toronto"), and [Church and Wellesley](/wiki/Church_and_Wellesley "Church and Wellesley"). From that point, the Toronto skyline extends northward along Yonge Street.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Old Toronto is also home to many historically wealthy residential enclaves, such as [Yorkville](/wiki/Yorkville,_Toronto "Yorkville, Toronto"), Rosedale, The Annex, Forest Hill, [Lawrence Park](/wiki/Lawrence_Park,_Toronto "Lawrence Park, Toronto"), [Lytton Park](/wiki/Lytton_Park "Lytton Park"), [Deer Park](/wiki/Deer_Park,_Toronto "Deer Park, Toronto"), [Moore Park](/wiki/Moore_Park,_Toronto "Moore Park, Toronto"), and Casa Loma, most stretching away from downtown to the north.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
East and west of downtown, neighbourhoods such as [Kensington Market](/wiki/Kensington_Market "Kensington Market"), [Chinatown](/wiki/Chinatown,_Toronto "Chinatown, Toronto"), [Leslieville](/wiki/Leslieville "Leslieville"), [Cabbagetown](/wiki/Cabbagetown,_Toronto "Cabbagetown, Toronto") and [Riverdale](/wiki/Riverdale,_Toronto "Riverdale, Toronto") are home to bustling commercial and cultural areas as well as communities of artists with studio lofts, with many middle- and upper-class professionals.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
Other neighbourhoods in the central city retain an ethnic identity, including two smaller Chinatowns, the [Greektown](/wiki/Greektown,_Toronto "Greektown, Toronto") area, [Little Italy](/wiki/Little_Italy,_Toronto "Little Italy, Toronto"), [Portugal Village](/wiki/Little_Portugal,_Toronto "Little Portugal, Toronto"), and [Little India](/wiki/Gerrard_Street_(Toronto) "Gerrard Street (Toronto)"), among others.[[113]](#cite_note-116)

#### Suburbs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Suburbs")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Crescent_Town_2022.jpg/250px-Crescent_Town_2022.jpg)](/wiki/File:Crescent_Town_2022.jpg)

[Crescent Town](/wiki/Crescent_Town "Crescent Town") and the surrounding area from the air. Crescent Town was a post-World War II suburban neighbourhood developed in [East York](/wiki/East_York "East York").

The inner suburbs are contained within the former municipalities of York and East York.[[114]](#cite_note-:0-117) These are mature and traditionally working-class areas, consisting primarily of post–World War I small, single-family homes and small apartment blocks.[[114]](#cite_note-:0-117) Neighbourhoods such as [Crescent Town](/wiki/Crescent_Town "Crescent Town"), [Thorncliffe Park](/wiki/Thorncliffe_Park "Thorncliffe Park"), [Flemingdon Park](/wiki/Flemingdon_Park "Flemingdon Park"), Weston, and [Oakwood Village](/wiki/Oakwood_Village "Oakwood Village") consist mainly of high-rise apartments, which are home to many new immigrant families. During the 2000s, many neighbourhoods became ethnically diverse and underwent [gentrification](/wiki/Gentrification "Gentrification") due to increasing population and a housing boom during the late 1990s and the early 21st century. The first neighbourhoods affected were [Leaside](/wiki/Leaside "Leaside") and [North Toronto](/wiki/North_Toronto "North Toronto"), gradually progressing into the western neighbourhoods in York.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/York_after_sunset_%282855524410%29.jpg/250px-York_after_sunset_%282855524410%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:York_after_sunset_(2855524410).jpg)

In an attempt to curb [suburban sprawl](/wiki/Urban_sprawl "Urban sprawl"), many suburban neighbourhoods in Toronto encouraged high-density populations by mixing housing lots with apartment buildings far from the downtown core.

The outer suburbs comprising the former municipalities of Etobicoke (west), Scarborough (east) and North York (north) largely retain the grid plan laid before post-war development.[[115]](#cite_note-118) Sections were long established and quickly growing towns before the suburban housing boom began and the emergence of metropolitan government, existing towns or villages such as Mimico, [Islington](/wiki/Islington-City_Centre_West "Islington-City Centre West") and [New Toronto](/wiki/New_Toronto "New Toronto") in Etobicoke; [Willowdale](/wiki/Willowdale,_Toronto "Willowdale, Toronto"), [Newtonbrook](/wiki/Newtonbrook "Newtonbrook") and [Downsview](/wiki/Downsview "Downsview") in North York; [Agincourt](/wiki/Agincourt,_Toronto "Agincourt, Toronto"), [Wexford](/wiki/Wexford,_Toronto "Wexford, Toronto") and [West Hill](/wiki/West_Hill,_Toronto "West Hill, Toronto") in Scarborough where suburban development boomed around or between these and other towns beginning in the late 1940s. Upscale neighbourhoods were built, such as the [Bridle Path](/wiki/Bridle_Path,_Toronto "Bridle Path, Toronto") in North York, the area surrounding the Scarborough Bluffs in [Guildwood](/wiki/Guildwood "Guildwood"), and most of central Etobicoke, such as [Humber Valley Village](/wiki/Humber_Valley_Village "Humber Valley Village"), and [The Kingsway](/wiki/The_Kingsway,_Toronto "The Kingsway, Toronto"). One of the largest and earliest "planned communities" was [Don Mills](/wiki/Don_Mills "Don Mills"), parts of which were first built in the 1950s.[[116]](#cite_note-119) Phased development, mixing single-detached housing with higher-density apartment blocks, became more popular as a suburban model of development. During the late 20th century, [North York City Centre](/wiki/North_York_City_Centre "North York City Centre") and [Scarborough City Centre](/wiki/Scarborough_City_Centre "Scarborough City Centre") developed separate downtown districts outside Downtown Toronto after the former boroughs were promoted to cities.[[117]](#cite_note-CHRON-120) High-rise development in these areas has given these former municipalities distinguishable skylines of their own, with high-density transit corridors serving them; some of these [developments are also transit-oriented](/wiki/Transit-oriented_development "Transit-oriented development").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Industrial

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Industrial")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/Toronto%27s_Distillery_District_in_2023_%2852716109425%29.jpg/250px-Toronto%27s_Distillery_District_in_2023_%2852716109425%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Toronto%27s_Distillery_District_in_2023_(52716109425).jpg)

The [Distillery District](/wiki/Distillery_District "Distillery District") holds the most extensive collection of preserved Victorian industrial architecture in North America.

In the 1800s, a thriving industrial area developed around Toronto Harbour and the lower Don River mouth, linked by rail and water to Canada and the United States. Examples included the Gooderham and Worts Distillery, Canadian Malting Company, the Toronto Rolling Mills, the Union Stockyards and the [Davies pork processing facility](/wiki/William_Davies_Company "William Davies Company") (the inspiration for the "Hogtown" nickname).[[118]](#cite_note-121)[[119]](#cite_note-122) This industrial area expanded west along the harbour and rail lines and was supplemented by the infilling of the marshlands on the east side of the harbour to create the Port Lands. A garment industry developed along lower Spadina Avenue, the "[Fashion District](/wiki/Fashion_District,_Toronto "Fashion District, Toronto")". Beginning in the late 19th century, industrial areas were set up on the outskirts, such as [West Toronto / The Junction](/wiki/The_Junction "The Junction"), where the Stockyards relocated in 1903.[[120]](#cite_note-stockyards-123) The Great Fire of 1904 destroyed a large amount of industry in the downtown. Some companies moved west along King Street, and some moved as far west as Dufferin Street, where the large [Massey-Harris](/wiki/Massey-Harris "Massey-Harris") farm equipment manufacturing complex was located.[[121]](#cite_note-124) Over time, pockets of industrial land mostly followed rail lines and later highway corridors as the city grew outwards. This trend continues to this day; the largest factories and distribution warehouses are in the suburban environs of [Peel](/wiki/Regional_Municipality_of_Peel "Regional Municipality of Peel") and [York](/wiki/Regional_Municipality_of_York "Regional Municipality of York") Regions, but also within the current city: Etobicoke (concentrated around [Pearson Airport](/wiki/Toronto_Pearson_International_Airport "Toronto Pearson International Airport")), North York, and Scarborough.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/Skyline%2C_2013_09_14_%2817%29.JPG/250px-Skyline%2C_2013_09_14_%2817%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:Skyline,_2013_09_14_(17).JPG)

The [West Don Lands](/wiki/West_Don_Lands "West Don Lands") is a [former industrial site](/wiki/Brownfield_land "Brownfield land") in downtown Toronto that has undergone redevelopment.

Many of Toronto's [former industrial sites](/wiki/Brownfield_land "Brownfield land") close to (or in) downtown have been redeveloped, including parts of the Toronto waterfront, the rail yards west of downtown, and [Liberty Village](/wiki/Liberty_Village "Liberty Village"), the Massey-Harris district and large-scale development is underway in the [West Don Lands](/wiki/West_Don_Lands "West Don Lands").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
The Gooderham & Worts Distillery produced spirits until 1990 and is preserved today as the "Distillery District", the largest and best-preserved collection of [Victorian](/wiki/Victorian_architecture "Victorian architecture") industrial architecture in North America.[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGibson2008-125) Some industry remains in the area, including the [Redpath Sugar Refinery](/wiki/Redpath_Sugar_Refinery "Redpath Sugar Refinery"). Similar areas that retain their industrial character but are now largely residential are the Fashion District, Corktown, and parts of South Riverdale and Leslieville. Toronto still has some active older industrial areas, such as [Brockton Village](/wiki/Brockton_Village "Brockton Village"), Mimico and New Toronto. In the west end of Old Toronto and York, the Weston/[Mount Dennis](/wiki/Mount_Dennis "Mount Dennis") and The Junction areas still contain factories, meat-packing facilities and rail yards close to medium-density residential. However, the Junction's Union Stockyards moved out of Toronto in 1994.[[120]](#cite_note-stockyards-123)

The brownfield industrial area of the Port Lands, on the east side of the harbour, is one area planned for redevelopment.[[123]](#cite_note-126) Formerly a marsh that was filled in to create industrial space, it was never intensely developed—its land unsuitable for large-scale development—because of flooding and unstable soil.[[124]](#cite_note-127)
It still contains numerous industrial uses, such as the [Portlands Energy Centre](/wiki/Portlands_Energy_Centre "Portlands Energy Centre") power plant, port facilities, movie and television production studios, concrete processing facilities, and low-density industrial facilities. The [Waterfront Toronto](/wiki/Waterfront_Toronto "Waterfront Toronto") agency has developed plans for a naturalized mouth to the Don River and to create a flood barrier around the Don, making more of the land on the harbour suitable for higher-value residential and commercial development.[[125]](#cite_note-128)
A former chemicals plant site along the Don River is slated to become a large commercial complex and transportation hub.[[126]](#cite_note-129)

### Architecture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Architecture")]

Main article: [Architecture of Toronto](/wiki/Architecture_of_Toronto "Architecture of Toronto")

See also: [List of oldest buildings and structures in Toronto](/wiki/List_of_oldest_buildings_and_structures_in_Toronto "List of oldest buildings and structures in Toronto") and [List of tallest buildings in Toronto](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Toronto "List of tallest buildings in Toronto")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/Sharp_Centre_for_Design.jpg/250px-Sharp_Centre_for_Design.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sharp_Centre_for_Design.jpg)

The Rosalie Sharp Centre for Design, an extension of [OCAD University](/wiki/OCAD_University "OCAD University")'s main building

Toronto's buildings vary in design and age, with many structures dating back to the early 19th century, while other prominent buildings were just newly built in the first decade of the 21st century.[[127]](#cite_note-130) Lawrence Richards, a member of the Faculty of Architecture at the [University of Toronto](/wiki/University_of_Toronto "University of Toronto"), has said, "Toronto is a new, brash, rag-tag place—a big mix of periods and styles."[[128]](#cite_note-131) [Bay-and-gable](/wiki/Bay-and-gable "Bay-and-gable") houses, mainly found in Old Toronto, are a distinct architectural feature of the city.

Toronto is a city with a substantial amount of skyscrapers and high-rise buildings. The city has [106 skyscrapers](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Toronto "List of tallest buildings in Toronto") taller than 150 metres (492 ft), the [16th-most](/wiki/List_of_cities_with_the_most_skyscrapers "List of cities with the most skyscrapers") in the world and the most in Canada by far.[[129]](#cite_note-132) There are currently three "[supertall](/wiki/Supertall "Supertall")" skyscrapers (taller than 300 metres (984 ft)) under construction in the city, and more have been approved.[[130]](#cite_note-133)[[131]](#cite_note-134) Defining the Toronto skyline is the [CN Tower](/wiki/CN_Tower "CN Tower"), a telecommunications and tourism hub. Completed in 1976 at a height of 553.33 metres (1,815 ft 5 in), it was the world's tallest[[132]](#cite_note-135) freestanding structure until 2007 when it was surpassed by [Burj Khalifa](/wiki/Burj_Khalifa "Burj Khalifa") in [Dubai](/wiki/Dubai "Dubai").[[133]](#cite_note-136)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Sunset_Toronto_Skyline_Panorama_Crop_from_Snake_Island.jpg/250px-Sunset_Toronto_Skyline_Panorama_Crop_from_Snake_Island.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sunset_Toronto_Skyline_Panorama_Crop_from_Snake_Island.jpg)

Sunset Toronto Skyline

Through the 1960s and 1970s, significant pieces of Toronto's architectural heritage were demolished to make way for redevelopment or parking. In contrast, since 2000, amid the [Canadian property bubble](/wiki/Canadian_property_bubble "Canadian property bubble"), Toronto has experienced a condo construction boom and architectural revival, with several buildings opened by world-renowned architects. [Daniel Libeskind](/wiki/Daniel_Libeskind "Daniel Libeskind")'s [Royal Ontario Museum](/wiki/Royal_Ontario_Museum "Royal Ontario Museum") addition, [Frank Gehry](/wiki/Frank_Gehry "Frank Gehry")'s remake of the [Art Gallery of Ontario](/wiki/Art_Gallery_of_Ontario "Art Gallery of Ontario"), and [Will Alsop](/wiki/Will_Alsop "Will Alsop")'s distinctive [OCAD University](/wiki/OCAD_University "OCAD University") expansion are among the city's new showpieces.[[134]](#cite_note-137) The mid-1800s Distillery District, on the eastern edge of downtown, has been redeveloped into a pedestrian-oriented arts, culture and entertainment neighbourhood.[[135]](#cite_note-138) This construction boom has some observers call the phenomenon the [Manhattanization](/wiki/Manhattanization "Manhattanization") of Toronto after [the densely built island borough](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan") of New York City.[[136]](#cite_note-139)

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Climate")]

The city of Toronto has a hot summer [humid continental climate](/wiki/Humid_continental_climate "Humid continental climate") ([Köppen](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"): *Dfa*),[[138]](#cite_note-Peel-141) though was on the threshold of a warm summer humid continental climate (*Dfb*) until the 20th century due to the [urban heat island](/wiki/Urban_heat_island "Urban heat island") but still found in the [metropolitan region](/wiki/Greater_Toronto_Area "Greater Toronto Area"),[[139]](#cite_note-142) with warm, humid summers and cold winters. According to the classification applied by [Natural Resources Canada](/wiki/Natural_Resources_Canada "Natural Resources Canada"), the city of Toronto is in plant hardiness zone 7a. Some suburbs and nearby towns have lower zone ratings.[[140]](#cite_note-143)[[141]](#cite_note-144)

The city experiences four distinct seasons, with considerable variance in length.[[142]](#cite_note-climatedriverv1-145) As a result of the rapid passage of weather systems (such as high- and low-pressure systems), the weather is variable from day to day in all seasons.[[142]](#cite_note-climatedriverv1-145) Owing to urbanization and its proximity to water, Toronto has a fairly low [diurnal temperature range](/wiki/Diurnal_temperature_variation "Diurnal temperature variation"). The denser urbanscape makes for warmer nights year-round; the average nighttime temperature is about 3.0 °C (5.4 °F) warmer in the city than in rural areas in all months.[[143]](#cite_note-climatedriverv3-146) However, it can be noticeably cooler on many spring and early summer afternoons under the influence of a lake breeze, since Lake Ontario is cool relative to the air during these seasons.[[143]](#cite_note-climatedriverv3-146) These lake breezes mostly occur in summer, bringing relief on hot days.[[143]](#cite_note-climatedriverv3-146) Other low-scale maritime effects on the climate include [lake-effect snow](/wiki/Lake-effect_snow "Lake-effect snow"), fog, and delaying of spring- and fall-like conditions, known as [seasonal lag](/wiki/Seasonal_lag "Seasonal lag").[[143]](#cite_note-climatedriverv3-146)

Winters are cold, with frequent snow.[[144]](#cite_note-climate-147) During the winter months, temperatures are usually below 0 °C (32 °F).[[144]](#cite_note-climate-147) Toronto winters sometimes feature cold snaps when maximum temperatures remain below −10 °C (14 °F), often made to feel colder by [wind chill](/wiki/Wind_chill "Wind chill"). Occasionally, they can drop below −25 °C (−13 °F).[[144]](#cite_note-climate-147) Snowstorms, sometimes mixed with ice and rain, can disrupt work and travel schedules while accumulating snow can fall anytime from November until mid-April. However, mild stretches also occur in most winters, melting accumulated snow. The summer months are characterized by very warm temperatures.[[144]](#cite_note-climate-147) Daytime temperatures are usually above 20 °C (68 °F), and often rise above 30 °C (86 °F).[[144]](#cite_note-climate-147) However, they can occasionally surpass 35 °C (95 °F) accompanied by high humidity. Spring and autumn are transitional seasons with generally mild or cool temperatures with alternating dry and wet periods.[[143]](#cite_note-climatedriverv3-146) Daytime temperatures average around 10 to 12 °C (50 to 54 °F) during these seasons.[[144]](#cite_note-climate-147)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/NorthYorkIceStorm2.JPG/250px-NorthYorkIceStorm2.JPG)](/wiki/File:NorthYorkIceStorm2.JPG)

Winters in Toronto are typically cold with frequent snowfall.

Precipitation is fairly evenly distributed throughout the year, but summer is usually the wettest season, the bulk falling during thunderstorms. The average yearly precipitation is 822.7 mm (32.4 in), with an average annual snowfall of 121.5 cm (48 in).[[145]](#cite_note-annex-148) Toronto experiences an average of 2,066 sunshine hours or 45 per cent of daylight hours, varying between a low of 28 per cent in December to 60 per cent in July.[[145]](#cite_note-annex-148)

Climate change has affected Toronto, and as a consequence, the Toronto City Council declared a climate emergency, setting a net-zero carbon emissions target by 2040 through the [TransformTO](/wiki/TransformTO "TransformTO") climate action plan.[[146]](#cite_note-149)

The highest temperature ever recorded in Toronto was 40.6 °C (105 °F) on July 8, 9 and 10, 1936,[[147]](#cite_note-July_1936-150) during the [1936 North American heat wave](/wiki/1936_North_American_heat_wave "1936 North American heat wave"). The coldest temperature ever recorded was −32.8 °C (−27 °F) on January 10, 1859.[[137]](#cite_note-Toronto_(City)-140)

### Parks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Parks")]

See also: [List of Toronto parks](/wiki/List_of_Toronto_parks "List of Toronto parks")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Rouge_National_Urban_Park-Finch_Meander_Trail-Toronto-Ontario.jpg/250px-Rouge_National_Urban_Park-Finch_Meander_Trail-Toronto-Ontario.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rouge_National_Urban_Park-Finch_Meander_Trail-Toronto-Ontario.jpg)

[Rouge National Urban Park](/wiki/Rouge_National_Urban_Park "Rouge National Urban Park") is a [national park](/wiki/National_parks_of_Canada "National parks of Canada") in [Scarborough](/wiki/Scarborough,_Ontario "Scarborough, Ontario")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/Toronto_City_Hall_%2825383892988%29.jpg/250px-Toronto_City_Hall_%2825383892988%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Toronto_City_Hall_(25383892988).jpg)

[Nathan Phillips Square](/wiki/Nathan_Phillips_Square "Nathan Phillips Square") is the city's main square. The square includes a reflecting pool that is converted into an [ice rink](/wiki/Ice_rink "Ice rink") during the winter.

Toronto has diverse public spaces, from city squares to public parks overlooking ravines. [Nathan Phillips Square](/wiki/Nathan_Phillips_Square "Nathan Phillips Square") is the city's main square in downtown, contains the [Toronto Sign](/wiki/Toronto_Sign "Toronto Sign"),[[149]](#cite_note-154) and forms the entrance to [City Hall](/wiki/Toronto_City_Hall "Toronto City Hall"). [Sankofa Square](/wiki/Sankofa_Square "Sankofa Square"), near City Hall, has also gained attention in recent years as one of the busiest gathering spots in the city. Other squares include [Maple Leaf Square](/wiki/Maple_Leaf_Square "Maple Leaf Square"), next to [Scotiabank Arena](/wiki/Scotiabank_Arena "Scotiabank Arena"),[[150]](#cite_note-155) and the civic squares at the former city halls of the defunct Metropolitan Toronto, most notably [Mel Lastman Square](/wiki/Mel_Lastman_Square "Mel Lastman Square") in North York.[[151]](#cite_note-156)[[152]](#cite_note-157)

There are many large downtown parks, which include [Allan Gardens](/wiki/Allan_Gardens "Allan Gardens"), [Christie Pits](/wiki/Christie_Pits "Christie Pits"), [Grange Park](/wiki/Grange_Park_(Toronto) "Grange Park (Toronto)"), [Little Norway Park](/wiki/Little_Norway_Park "Little Norway Park"), [Moss Park](/wiki/Moss_Park "Moss Park"), [Queen's Park](/wiki/Queen%27s_Park_(Toronto) "Queen's Park (Toronto)"), [Riverdale Park](/wiki/Riverdale_Park_(Toronto) "Riverdale Park (Toronto)") and [Trinity Bellwoods Park](/wiki/Trinity_Bellwoods_Park "Trinity Bellwoods Park"). An almost-hidden park is the compact [Cloud Gardens](/wiki/Cloud_Gardens "Cloud Gardens"),[[153]](#cite_note-158) which has both open areas and a glassed-in greenhouse, near Queen Street and Yonge Street. South of downtown are two large parks on the waterfront: Tommy Thompson Park on the [Leslie Street Spit](/wiki/Leslie_Street_Spit "Leslie Street Spit"), which has a nature preserve and is open on weekends, and the [Toronto Islands](/wiki/Toronto_Islands "Toronto Islands"), accessible from downtown by ferry.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fc/Spring_has_arrived_at_James_Gardens%21_Toronto%2C_May_13th%2C_2018_%2842086291601%29.jpg/250px-Spring_has_arrived_at_James_Gardens%21_Toronto%2C_May_13th%2C_2018_%2842086291601%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Spring_has_arrived_at_James_Gardens!_Toronto,_May_13th,_2018_(42086291601).jpg)

[James Gardens](/wiki/James_Gardens "James Gardens"), a botanical garden operated by [Toronto Parks and Recreation Division](/wiki/Toronto_Parks_and_Recreation_Division "Toronto Parks and Recreation Division")

Large parks in the outer areas managed by the city include [High Park](/wiki/High_Park "High Park"), [Humber Bay Park](/wiki/Humber_Bay_Park "Humber Bay Park"), [Centennial Park](/wiki/Centennial_Park_(Toronto) "Centennial Park (Toronto)"), [Downsview Park](/wiki/Downsview_Park "Downsview Park"), [Guild Park and Gardens](/wiki/Guild_Park_and_Gardens "Guild Park and Gardens"), [Sunnybrook Park](/wiki/Sunnybrook_Park "Sunnybrook Park") and [Morningside Park](/wiki/Morningside_Park_(Toronto) "Morningside Park (Toronto)").[[154]](#cite_note-159) Toronto also operates several public golf courses. Most ravine lands and river bank floodplains in Toronto are public parklands. After [Hurricane Hazel](/wiki/Hurricane_Hazel "Hurricane Hazel") in 1954, construction of buildings on floodplains was outlawed, and private lands were bought for conservation. In 1999, Downsview Park, a former military base in North York, initiated an international design competition to realize its vision of creating Canada's first [urban park](/wiki/Urban_park "Urban park"). Approximately 8,000 hectares (20,000 acres), or 12.5 per cent of Toronto's land base, is maintained parkland.[[155]](#cite_note-gnpark-160) Morningside Park in Scarborough is the largest park managed by the city, which is 241.46 hectares (596.7 acres) in size.[[155]](#cite_note-gnpark-160)

In addition to public parks managed by the municipal government, parts of [Rouge National Urban Park](/wiki/Rouge_National_Urban_Park "Rouge National Urban Park"), the largest urban park in North America, is in the [eastern portion](/wiki/Rouge,_Toronto "Rouge, Toronto") of Toronto. Managed by [Parks Canada](/wiki/Parks_Canada "Parks Canada"), the [national park](/wiki/National_parks_of_Canada "National parks of Canada") is centred around the Rouge River and encompasses several municipalities in the Greater Toronto Area.[[156]](#cite_note-161)

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Demographics")]
