In the [2021 Census of Population](/wiki/2021_Canadian_census "2021 Canadian census") conducted by [Statistics Canada](/wiki/Statistics_Canada "Statistics Canada"), Toronto had a population of 2,794,356 living in 1,160,892 of its 1,253,238 total private dwellings, a change of 2.3 per cent from its 2016 population of 2,731,571. With a land area of 631.1 km2 (243.7 sq mi), it had a population density of 4,427.8/km2 (11,467.8/sq mi) in 2021.[[163]](#cite_note-2021census-168)

At the [census metropolitan area](/wiki/Census_metropolitan_area "Census metropolitan area") (CMA) level in the 2021 census, the Toronto CMA had a population of 6,202,225 living in 2,262,473 of its 2,394,205 total private dwellings, a change of 4.6 per cent from its 2016 population of 5,928,040. With a land area of 5,902.75 km2 (2,279.06 sq mi), it had a population density of 1,050.7/km2 (2,721.4/sq mi) in 2021.[[164]](#cite_note-2021censusCMA-169)

The city is the anchor of the [Golden Horseshoe](/wiki/Golden_Horseshoe#Demographics "Golden Horseshoe"), an urban agglomeration surrounding the western end of [Lake Ontario](/wiki/Lake_Ontario "Lake Ontario") with a population of 9,765,188 people in 2021 and an estimated population of 11,139,265 people in 2024.[[165]](#cite_note-:3-170)

In 2016, persons aged 14 years and under made up 14.5 per cent of the population, and those aged 65 and over made up 15.6 per cent.[[166]](#cite_note-sc-profile-to-171) The [median](/wiki/Median "Median") age was 39.3 years.[[166]](#cite_note-sc-profile-to-171) The city's gender population is 48 per cent male and 52 per cent female.[[166]](#cite_note-sc-profile-to-171) Women outnumber men in all age groups 15 and older.[[166]](#cite_note-sc-profile-to-171)

The [2021 census](/wiki/2021_Canadian_census "2021 Canadian census") reported that [immigrants](/wiki/Immigration_to_Canada "Immigration to Canada") (individuals born outside Canada) comprise 1,286,145 persons or 46.6 per cent of the total population of Toronto. Of the total immigrant population, the top countries of origin were Philippines (132,980 persons or 10.3%), China (129,750 persons or 10.1%), India (102,155 persons or 7.9%), Sri Lanka (47,895 persons or 3.7%), Jamaica (42,655 persons or 3.3%), Italy (37,705 persons or 2.9%), Iran (37,185 persons or 2.9%), Hong Kong (36,855 persons or 2.9%), United Kingdom (35,585 persons or 2.8%), and Portugal (34,360 persons or 2.7%).[[167]](#cite_note-2021censusB-172)

The city's foreign-born persons comprised 47 per cent of the population,[[168]](#cite_note-sc-geo-profile-to-173) compared to 49.9 per cent in 2006.[[169]](#cite_note-2006diversity-174) According to the [United Nations Development Programme](/wiki/United_Nations_Development_Programme "United Nations Development Programme"), Toronto has the second-highest percentage of constant foreign-born population among world cities, after [Miami, Florida](/wiki/Miami "Miami"). While Miami's foreign-born population has traditionally consisted primarily of [Cubans](/wiki/Cuba "Cuba") and other Latin Americans, no single nationality or culture dominates Toronto's immigrant population, placing it among the most diverse cities in the world.[[169]](#cite_note-2006diversity-174) In 2010, it was estimated over 100,000 immigrants arrived in the Greater Toronto Area each year.[[170]](#cite_note-175)

### Race and ethnicity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Race and ethnicity")]

In 2016, the three most commonly reported ethnic origins overall were [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_Canadians "Chinese Canadians") (332,830 or 12.5 per cent), [English](/wiki/English_Canadians "English Canadians") (331,890 or 12.3 per cent) and [Canadian](/wiki/Canadian_ethnicity "Canadian ethnicity") (323,175 or 12.0 per cent).[[168]](#cite_note-sc-geo-profile-to-173) Common regions of ethnic origin were European (47.9 per cent), Asian (including Middle-Eastern – 40.1 per cent), African (5.5 per cent), Latin/Central/South American (4.2 per cent), and North American aboriginal (1.2 per cent).[[168]](#cite_note-sc-geo-profile-to-173)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/Toronto_Population_Pyramid%2C_2021.png/330px-Toronto_Population_Pyramid%2C_2021.png)](/wiki/File:Toronto_Population_Pyramid,_2021.png)

Population pyramid of Toronto from the [2021 Canadian census](/wiki/2021_Canadian_census "2021 Canadian census")

In 2016, 51.5 per cent of the residents of the city proper belonged to a [visible minority](/wiki/Visible_minority "Visible minority") group, compared to 49.1 per cent in 2011,[[168]](#cite_note-sc-geo-profile-to-173)[[171]](#cite_note-www12.statcan.gc.ca-176) and 13.6 per cent in 1981.[[172]](#cite_note-177) The largest visible minority groups were [South Asian](/wiki/South_Asian_Canadians "South Asian Canadians") (Indian, Pakistani, Sri Lankan at 338,960 or 12.6 per cent), [East Asian](/wiki/East_Asian_Canadians "East Asian Canadians") ([Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_Canadians "Chinese Canadians") at 332,830 or 12.5 per cent), and [Black](/wiki/Black_Canadians "Black Canadians") (239,850 or 8.9 per cent).[[168]](#cite_note-sc-geo-profile-to-173) Visible minorities are projected to increase to 63 per cent of the city's population by 2031.[[173]](#cite_note-Visible_minority-178)

This diversity is reflected in Toronto's ethnic neighbourhoods, which include the Chinatowns, [Corso Italia](/wiki/Corso_Italia_(Toronto) "Corso Italia (Toronto)"), [Greektown](/wiki/Greektown,_Toronto "Greektown, Toronto"), [Kensington Market](/wiki/Kensington_Market "Kensington Market"), [Koreatown](/wiki/Koreatown,_Toronto "Koreatown, Toronto"), [Little India](/wiki/Gerrard_India_Bazaar "Gerrard India Bazaar"), [Little Italy](/wiki/Little_Italy,_Toronto "Little Italy, Toronto"), [Little Jamaica](/wiki/Little_Jamaica "Little Jamaica"), [Little Portugal](/wiki/Little_Portugal,_Toronto "Little Portugal, Toronto") and [Roncesvalles](/wiki/Roncesvalles,_Toronto "Roncesvalles, Toronto") (Polish community).[[174]](#cite_note-179)

### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Religion")]

According to the [2021 census](/wiki/2021_Canadian_census "2021 Canadian census"), religious groups in Toronto included:[[167]](#cite_note-2021censusB-172)

* [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity_in_Canada "Christianity in Canada") (1,274,450 persons or 46.2%)
* [No religion and secular perspectives](/wiki/Irreligion_in_Canada "Irreligion in Canada") (845,615 persons or 30.6%)
* [Islam](/wiki/Islam_in_Canada "Islam in Canada") (264,155 persons or 9.6%)
* [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism_in_Canada "Hinduism in Canada") (171,980 persons or 6.2%)
* [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism_in_Canada "Judaism in Canada") (99,390 persons or 3.6%)
* [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism_in_Canada "Buddhism in Canada") (62,475 persons or 2.3%)
* [Sikhism](/wiki/Sikhism_in_Canada "Sikhism in Canada") (21,545 persons or 0.8%)
* [Indigenous Spirituality](/wiki/Mythologies_of_the_Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Mythologies of the Indigenous peoples of the Americas") (935 persons or <0.1%)
* Other (20,730 persons or 0.8%)

### Language

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Language")]

English is the predominant language spoken by Torontonians, with approximately 95 per cent of residents having proficiency in it, although only 54.7 per cent of Torontonians reported English as their mother tongue.[[176]](#cite_note-langcen-181) Multicultural Toronto English, or simply [Toronto slang](/wiki/Toronto_slang "Toronto slang"), is a dialect of English found primarily among young people in the Greater Toronto Area.[[177]](#cite_note-182) English is one of two [official languages of Canada](/wiki/Official_bilingualism_in_Canada "Official bilingualism in Canada"), with the other being French. Approximately 1.6 per cent of Torontonians reported French as their mother tongue, although 9.1 per cent reported being bilingual in both official languages.[[176]](#cite_note-langcen-181) In addition to services provided by the federal government, provincial services in Toronto are available in both official languages as a result of the *[French Language Services Act](/wiki/French_Language_Services_Act_(Ontario) "French Language Services Act (Ontario)")*.[[178]](#cite_note-183) Approximately 4.9 per cent of Torontonians reported having no knowledge in either of the official languages of the country.[[176]](#cite_note-langcen-181)

Because the city is also home to many other languages, municipal services, most notably its [9-1-1](/wiki/911_(emergency_telephone_number) "911 (emergency telephone number)") [emergency telephone service](/wiki/Emergency_telephone_number "Emergency telephone number"),[[f]](#cite_note-184) is equipped to respond in over 150 languages.[[179]](#cite_note-911service-185)[[180]](#cite_note-lan3-186) In the [2001 Canadian census](/wiki/2001_Canadian_census "2001 Canadian census"), the collective [varieties of Chinese](/wiki/Varieties_of_Chinese "Varieties of Chinese") and [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language") are the most widely spoken languages at work after English.[[181]](#cite_note-lan-187)[[182]](#cite_note-lang2-188) Approximately 55 per cent of respondents who reported proficiency in a Chinese language reported knowledge of [Mandarin](/wiki/Mandarin_Chinese "Mandarin Chinese") in the 2016 census.[[176]](#cite_note-langcen-181)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Economy")]

Main article: [Economy of Toronto](/wiki/Economy_of_Toronto "Economy of Toronto")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/Toronto_from_above_at_night.jpg/250px-Toronto_from_above_at_night.jpg)](/wiki/File:Toronto_from_above_at_night.jpg)

View looking towards Toronto's [Financial District](/wiki/Financial_District,_Toronto "Financial District, Toronto")

Toronto is an international centre for business and finance. Generally considered the financial and industrial capital of Canada, Toronto has a high concentration of banks and brokerage firms on [Bay Street](/wiki/Bay_Street "Bay Street") in the [Financial District](/wiki/Financial_District,_Toronto "Financial District, Toronto"). The [Toronto Stock Exchange](/wiki/Toronto_Stock_Exchange "Toronto Stock Exchange") is the world's [seventh-largest](/wiki/List_of_major_stock_exchanges "List of major stock exchanges") stock exchange by market capitalization.[[183]](#cite_note-189) The five largest financial institutions of Canada, collectively known as the [Big Five](/wiki/Big_Five_banks_of_Canada "Big Five banks of Canada"), all have their global corporate headquarters in Toronto, alongside Canada's major insurance giants.[[184]](#cite_note-190)[[185]](#cite_note-191)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c4/Frozen_Toronto_harbour_%284675522485%29.jpg/250px-Frozen_Toronto_harbour_%284675522485%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frozen_Toronto_harbour_(4675522485).jpg)

[Lake freighters](/wiki/Lake_freighter "Lake freighter") moored in the [Port of Toronto](/wiki/Port_of_Toronto "Port of Toronto")

The city is an important centre for the media, publishing, telecommunication, information technology and film production industries; it is home to [Bell Media](/wiki/Bell_Media "Bell Media"), [Rogers Communications](/wiki/Rogers_Communications "Rogers Communications"), and [Torstar](/wiki/Torstar "Torstar"). Other prominent Canadian corporations and Canadian subsidiaries of international corporations in the Greater Toronto Area include [Magna International](/wiki/Magna_International "Magna International"), [Pizza Pizza](/wiki/Pizza_Pizza "Pizza Pizza"), [Mr. Sub](/wiki/Mr._Sub "Mr. Sub"), [Celestica](/wiki/Celestica "Celestica"), [Manulife](/wiki/Manulife "Manulife"), [Sun Life Financial](/wiki/Sun_Life_Financial "Sun Life Financial"), [Toyota Canada Inc.](/wiki/Toyota_Canada_Inc. "Toyota Canada Inc."), and major hotel companies and operators, such as [Four Seasons Hotels](/wiki/Four_Seasons_Hotels_and_Resorts "Four Seasons Hotels and Resorts") and [Fairmont Hotels & Resorts](/wiki/Fairmont_Hotels_%26_Resorts "Fairmont Hotels & Resorts").[[186]](#cite_note-192)

Although much of the region's manufacturing activities occur outside the city limits, Toronto continues to be a wholesale and distribution point for the industrial sector. The city's strategic position along the [Quebec City–Windsor Corridor](/wiki/Quebec_City%E2%80%93Windsor_Corridor "Quebec City–Windsor Corridor") within the [Great Lakes megalopolis](/wiki/Great_Lakes_megalopolis "Great Lakes megalopolis") and its road and rail connections help support the nearby production of motor vehicles, iron, steel, food, machinery, chemicals and paper. The completion of the [St. Lawrence Seaway](/wiki/St._Lawrence_Seaway "St. Lawrence Seaway") in 1959 gave ships access to the [Great Lakes](/wiki/Great_Lakes "Great Lakes") from the [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean").

Toronto's unemployment rate was 6.7 per cent as of July 2016.[[187]](#cite_note-193) According to the website Numbeo, Toronto's cost of living plus rent index was second highest in Canada (of 31 cities).[[188]](#cite_note-194) The local purchasing power was the sixth lowest in Canada, mid-2017.[[189]](#cite_note-195) The average monthly social assistance caseload for January to October 2014 was 92,771. The number of impoverished seniors increased from 10.5 per cent in 2011 to 12.1 per cent in 2014. Toronto's 2013 child poverty rate was 28.6 per cent, the highest among large Canadian cities of 500,000 or more residents.[[190]](#cite_note-196)

### Bay Street

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Bay Street")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Toronto_Financial_District_August_2017.jpg/250px-Toronto_Financial_District_August_2017.jpg)](/wiki/File:Toronto_Financial_District_August_2017.jpg)

Buildings in the [Financial District](/wiki/Financial_District,_Toronto "Financial District, Toronto"), including the operational headquarters of three major Canadian banks

The [Financial District](/wiki/Financial_District,_Toronto "Financial District, Toronto") in Toronto centres on [Bay Street](/wiki/Bay_Street "Bay Street"), the equivalent to [Wall Street](/wiki/Wall_Street "Wall Street") in New York.[[191]](#cite_note-197) The city hosts the headquarters of all five of Canada's largest banks, [Royal Bank of Canada](/wiki/Royal_Bank_of_Canada "Royal Bank of Canada"), [Toronto-Dominion Bank](/wiki/Toronto-Dominion_Bank "Toronto-Dominion Bank"), [Scotiabank](/wiki/Scotiabank "Scotiabank"), [Bank of Montreal](/wiki/Bank_of_Montreal "Bank of Montreal") and [Canadian Imperial Bank of Commerce](/wiki/Canadian_Imperial_Bank_of_Commerce "Canadian Imperial Bank of Commerce"), and was ranked as the safest banking system in the world between 2007 and 2014 according to the World Economic Forum.[[114]](#cite_note-:0-117) Toronto's economy has seen a steady growth boom thanks to many corporations relocating their Canadian headquarters into the city and Canada's growing cultural significance, resulting in several companies setting up shop in Toronto.

### Media and entertainment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Media and entertainment")]

Main article: [Media in Toronto](/wiki/Media_in_Toronto "Media in Toronto")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/Omni-City-33Dundas.jpg/250px-Omni-City-33Dundas.jpg)](/wiki/File:Omni-City-33Dundas.jpg)

[33 Dundas Street East](/wiki/33_Dundas_Street_East "33 Dundas Street East") is a former studio complex used by [Citytv](/wiki/Citytv "Citytv") and [Omni Television](/wiki/Omni_Television "Omni Television")

Toronto is Canada's largest media market,[[192]](#cite_note-198) and has four conventional dailies, two [alt-weeklies](/wiki/Alternative_newspaper "Alternative newspaper"), and three free commuter papers in a greater metropolitan area of about 6 million inhabitants. The *[Toronto Star](/wiki/Toronto_Star "Toronto Star")* and the *[Toronto Sun](/wiki/Toronto_Sun "Toronto Sun")* are the prominent daily city newspapers, while national dailies *[The Globe and Mail](/wiki/The_Globe_and_Mail "The Globe and Mail")* and the *[National Post](/wiki/National_Post "National Post")* are also headquartered in the city.[[193]](#cite_note-199) The *Toronto Star*, *The Globe and Mail*, and *National Post* are broadsheet newspapers. Several magazines and local newspapers cover Toronto, including *[Now](/wiki/Now_(newspaper) "Now (newspaper)")* and *[Toronto Life](/wiki/Toronto_Life "Toronto Life")*, while numerous magazines are produced in Toronto, such as *[Canadian Business](/wiki/Canadian_Business "Canadian Business")*, *[Chatelaine](/wiki/Chatelaine_(magazine) "Chatelaine (magazine)")*, *[Flare](/wiki/Flare_(magazine) "Flare (magazine)")* and *[Maclean's](/wiki/Maclean%27s "Maclean's")*. [Daily Hive](/wiki/Daily_Hive "Daily Hive"), Western Canada's largest online-only publication, opened its Toronto office in 2016 after acquiring Torontoist from [Gothamist](/wiki/Gothamist "Gothamist").[[194]](#cite_note-200) Toronto contains the headquarters of the major English-language Canadian television networks [CBC](/wiki/CBC_Television "CBC Television"), [CTV](/wiki/CTV_Television_Network "CTV Television Network"), [Citytv](/wiki/Citytv "Citytv"), [Global](/wiki/Global_Television_Network "Global Television Network"), [The Sports Network](/wiki/The_Sports_Network "The Sports Network") (TSN) and [Sportsnet](/wiki/Sportsnet "Sportsnet").

#### Film production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Film production")]

See also: [Hollywood North](/wiki/Hollywood_North "Hollywood North")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/The_Boys_Season_12_filmed_at_Pecaut_Square_Toronto.jpg/250px-The_Boys_Season_12_filmed_at_Pecaut_Square_Toronto.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Boys_Season_12_filmed_at_Pecaut_Square_Toronto.jpg)

Film production for *[The Boys](/wiki/The_Boys_(TV_series) "The Boys (TV series)")* at [Pecaut Square](/wiki/Pecaut_Square "Pecaut Square") in 2019

Toronto is one of the centres of [Canada's film and television industry](/wiki/Cinema_of_Canada "Cinema of Canada") due in part to the lower cost of production in Canada. The city's streets and landmarks are seen in a variety of films, mimicking the scenes of American cities such as [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago") and [New York](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"). The city provides diverse settings and neighbourhoods to shoot films with production facilitated by Toronto's Film and Television Office. Lower Bay station, the abandoned lower platform of Bay station, is used for filming subway scenes. Toronto's film industry has extended beyond the [Toronto CMA](/wiki/Greater_Toronto_Area#Toronto_CMA "Greater Toronto Area") into adjoining cities such as [Hamilton](/wiki/Hamilton,_Ontario "Hamilton, Ontario") and [Oshawa](/wiki/Oshawa "Oshawa").[[195]](#cite_note-201)

### Real estate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Real estate")]

Real estate is a major force in the city's economy; Toronto is home to some of the nation's—and the world's—most expensive real estate, especially since the [Canadian property bubble](/wiki/Canadian_property_bubble "Canadian property bubble"). The Toronto Regional Real Estate Board (TRREB), formerly the Toronto Real Estate Board, is a non-profit professional association of registered [real estate](/wiki/Real_estate "Real estate") brokers and salespeople in Toronto, and parts of the [Greater Toronto Area](/wiki/Greater_Toronto_Area "Greater Toronto Area").[[196]](#cite_note-who_we_are-202) TRREB was formed in 1920.[[196]](#cite_note-who_we_are-202) Many large [real estate investment trusts](/wiki/Real_estate_investment_trust "Real estate investment trust") are based in Toronto.

### Technology and biotech

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Technology and biotech")]

Toronto is a large hub of the Canadian and global technology industry, generating $52 billion in revenues annually. In 2017, Toronto tech firms offered almost 30,000 jobs, which is higher than the combination of San Francisco Bay area, Seattle and Washington, D.C.[[197]](#cite_note-203) The area bound between the Greater Toronto Area, the [region of Waterloo](/wiki/Regional_Municipality_of_Waterloo "Regional Municipality of Waterloo") and the city of [Hamilton](/wiki/Hamilton,_Ontario "Hamilton, Ontario") was termed a "digital corridor" by the Branham Group,[[198]](#cite_note-204) a region highly concentrated with technology companies and jobs similar to [Silicon Valley](/wiki/Silicon_Valley "Silicon Valley") in California.[[199]](#cite_note-205) Toronto is home to a large startup ecosystem and is the third-largest center for information and communications technology in North America, behind [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") and the Silicon Valley.[[200]](#cite_note-206) In 2023, the city was ranked as the 17th best startup scene in the world.[[201]](#cite_note-207)

### Tourism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Tourism")]

Main article: [Tourism in Toronto](/wiki/Tourism_in_Toronto "Tourism in Toronto")

See also: [Hotels in Toronto](/wiki/Hotels_in_Toronto "Hotels in Toronto")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/Kensington_Market_Toronto_August_2017_01.jpg/250px-Kensington_Market_Toronto_August_2017_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kensington_Market_Toronto_August_2017_01.jpg)

[Kensington Market](/wiki/Kensington_Market "Kensington Market"), a neighbourhood that is also partly an outdoor market

In 2018, 27.5 million tourists visited Toronto, generating $10.3 billion (~$12.6 billion in 2024) in economic activity.[[202]](#cite_note-208) The [Toronto Eaton Centre](/wiki/Toronto_Eaton_Centre "Toronto Eaton Centre") receives over 47 million visitors per year.[[203]](#cite_note-209) Other commercial areas popular with tourists include the [Path](/wiki/Path_(Toronto) "Path (Toronto)") network, which is the world's largest[[204]](#cite_note-210) underground shopping complex, as well as [Kensington Market](/wiki/Kensington_Market "Kensington Market") and [St. Lawrence Market](/wiki/St._Lawrence_Market "St. Lawrence Market").[[205]](#cite_note-211) The [Toronto Islands](/wiki/Toronto_Islands "Toronto Islands") are close to downtown Toronto and do not permit private motor vehicles beyond the airport. Other tourist attractions include the CN Tower, [Casa Loma](/wiki/Casa_Loma "Casa Loma"), Toronto's theatres and musicals, Sankofa Square, and [Ripley's Aquarium of Canada](/wiki/Ripley%27s_Aquarium_of_Canada "Ripley's Aquarium of Canada").

The Royal Ontario Museum is a museum of world culture and [natural history](/wiki/Natural_history "Natural history"). The [Toronto Zoo](/wiki/Toronto_Zoo "Toronto Zoo")[[206]](#cite_note-aboutzoo-212)[[207]](#cite_note-zoo-213) is home to over 5,000 animals representing over 460 distinct species. The Art Gallery of Ontario contains an extensive collection of Canadian, European, African and [contemporary artwork](/wiki/Contemporary_art "Contemporary art"). Also, it hosts exhibits from museums and galleries from all over the world. The [Gardiner Museum](/wiki/Gardiner_Museum "Gardiner Museum") of ceramic art is the only museum in Canada entirely devoted to ceramics, and the Museum's collection contains more than 2,900 ceramic works from Asia, the Americas, and Europe. The city also hosts the [Bata Shoe Museum](/wiki/Bata_Shoe_Museum "Bata Shoe Museum") and [Textile Museum of Canada](/wiki/Textile_Museum_of_Canada "Textile Museum of Canada"). The [Ontario Science Centre](/wiki/Ontario_Science_Centre "Ontario Science Centre") is being relocated from its original Don Mills location within Toronto to the site of [Ontario Place](/wiki/Ontario_Place "Ontario Place").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/Art_Gallery_of_Ontario_%2838637506756%29.jpg/250px-Art_Gallery_of_Ontario_%2838637506756%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Art_Gallery_of_Ontario_(38637506756).jpg)

The southern façade of the [Art Gallery of Ontario](/wiki/Art_Gallery_of_Ontario "Art Gallery of Ontario")

Other prominent art galleries and museums include the [Design Exchange](/wiki/Design_Exchange "Design Exchange"),[[208]](#cite_note-214) the [TIFF Lightbox](/wiki/TIFF_Lightbox "TIFF Lightbox"), the [Museum of Contemporary Art Toronto Canada](/wiki/Museum_of_Contemporary_Art_Toronto_Canada "Museum of Contemporary Art Toronto Canada"), the [Institute for Contemporary Culture](/wiki/Institute_for_Contemporary_Culture "Institute for Contemporary Culture"), the [Toronto Sculpture Garden](/wiki/Toronto_Sculpture_Garden "Toronto Sculpture Garden"), the [CBC Museum](/wiki/CBC_Museum "CBC Museum"), the [Redpath Sugar Museum](/wiki/Redpath_Sugar_Museum "Redpath Sugar Museum"), the [University of Toronto Art Centre](/wiki/University_College,_Toronto "University College, Toronto"), [Hart House](/wiki/Hart_House_(University_of_Toronto) "Hart House (University of Toronto)"), the TD Gallery of Inuit Art, [Little Canada](/wiki/Little_Canada_(attraction) "Little Canada (attraction)") and the [Aga Khan Museum](/wiki/Aga_Khan_Museum "Aga Khan Museum"). The city also runs its own museums, which include the [Spadina House](/wiki/Spadina_House "Spadina House").[[209]](#cite_note-215) The [Don Valley Brick Works](/wiki/Don_Valley_Brick_Works "Don Valley Brick Works") is a former industrial site that opened in 1889 and was partly restored as a park and heritage site in 1996, with further restoration being completed in stages since then. The [Canadian National Exhibition](/wiki/Canadian_National_Exhibition "Canadian National Exhibition") ("The Ex") is held annually at Exhibition Place and is the oldest annual fair in the world. The Ex has an average attendance of 1.25 million.[[210]](#cite_note-216)

City shopping areas include the Yorkville neighbourhood, [Queen West](/wiki/Queen_Street_(Toronto) "Queen Street (Toronto)"), [Harbourfront](/wiki/Harbourfront,_Toronto "Harbourfront, Toronto"), the [Entertainment District](/wiki/Toronto_Entertainment_District "Toronto Entertainment District"), the Financial District, and the St. Lawrence Market neighbourhood.[[211]](#cite_note-217)[[212]](#cite_note-218) The [Eaton Centre](/wiki/Toronto_Eaton_Centre "Toronto Eaton Centre") is Toronto's most popular tourist attraction with over 52 million visitors annually.[[213]](#cite_note-219)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Hockey_Hall_of_Fame_building%2C_Toronto%2C_South_view_20170417_1.jpg/250px-Hockey_Hall_of_Fame_building%2C_Toronto%2C_South_view_20170417_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hockey_Hall_of_Fame_building,_Toronto,_South_view_20170417_1.jpg)

The [Hockey Hall of Fame](/wiki/Hockey_Hall_of_Fame "Hockey Hall of Fame") is a museum dedicated to [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey"), as well as a [Hall of Fame](/wiki/List_of_halls_and_walks_of_fame "List of halls and walks of fame")

Toronto is also home to Casa Loma, the former estate of [Sir Henry Pellatt](/wiki/Henry_Pellatt "Henry Pellatt"), a prominent Toronto financier, industrialist and military man. Other notable neighbourhoods and attractions in Toronto include [The Beaches](/wiki/The_Beaches,_Toronto "The Beaches, Toronto"), the Toronto Islands, [Kensington Market](/wiki/Kensington_Market "Kensington Market"), Fort York, and the [Hockey Hall of Fame](/wiki/Hockey_Hall_of_Fame "Hockey Hall of Fame").[[214]](#cite_note-220)[[215]](#cite_note-221)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Education")]

Main article: [Education in Toronto](/wiki/Education_in_Toronto "Education in Toronto")

### Primary and secondary education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Primary and secondary education")]

Further information: [List of secondary schools in Ontario § City of Toronto](/wiki/List_of_secondary_schools_in_Ontario#City_of_Toronto "List of secondary schools in Ontario")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/TorontoDistrictSchoolBoardEducationCentre_-_2015May30.jpg/250px-TorontoDistrictSchoolBoardEducationCentre_-_2015May30.jpg)](/wiki/File:TorontoDistrictSchoolBoardEducationCentre_-_2015May30.jpg)

Headquarters for the [Toronto District School Board](/wiki/Toronto_District_School_Board "Toronto District School Board"), one of four public school boards that operate in the city

There are four [public](/wiki/State_school "State school") [school boards](/wiki/School_board "School board") that provide [elementary](/wiki/Primary_education "Primary education") and [secondary education](/wiki/Secondary_education "Secondary education") in Toronto, the [Conseil scolaire catholique MonAvenir](/wiki/Conseil_scolaire_catholique_MonAvenir "Conseil scolaire catholique MonAvenir"), the [Conseil scolaire Viamonde](/wiki/Conseil_scolaire_Viamonde "Conseil scolaire Viamonde") (CSV), the [Toronto Catholic District School Board](/wiki/Toronto_Catholic_District_School_Board "Toronto Catholic District School Board") (TCDSB), and the [Toronto District School Board](/wiki/Toronto_District_School_Board "Toronto District School Board") (TDSB). CSV and TDSB are [secular](/wiki/Secular_education "Secular education") public school boards, whereas MonAvenir and TCDSB are [separate](/wiki/Separate_school "Separate school") public school boards. CSV and MonAvenir are French [first language](/wiki/First_language "First language") school boards, whereas TCDSB and TDSB are English first language school boards.

TDSB operates the most schools among the four Toronto-based school boards, with 451 elementary schools, 105 secondary schools, and five [adult learning centres](/wiki/Adult_high_school "Adult high school").[[216]](#cite_note-222) TCDSB operates 163 elementary schools, 29 secondary schools, three combined institutions, and one adult learning centre. CSV operates 11 elementary schools, and three secondary schools in the city.[[217]](#cite_note-223) MonAvenir operates nine elementary schools,[[218]](#cite_note-224) and three secondary schools in Toronto.[[219]](#cite_note-225)

### Postsecondary education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Postsecondary education")]

There are several public universities and colleges based in Toronto. The city is also home to several [supplementary schools](/wiki/Supplementary_school "Supplementary school"), [seminaries](/wiki/Seminary "Seminary"), and [vocational schools](/wiki/Vocational_school "Vocational school"). Examples of such institutions include [The Royal Conservatory of Music](/wiki/The_Royal_Conservatory_of_Music "The Royal Conservatory of Music"), which includes [the Glenn Gould School](/wiki/The_Glenn_Gould_School "The Glenn Gould School"); the [Canadian Film Centre](/wiki/Canadian_Film_Centre "Canadian Film Centre"), a media training institute founded by filmmaker [Norman Jewison](/wiki/Norman_Jewison "Norman Jewison"); and [Tyndale University](/wiki/Tyndale_University "Tyndale University"), a Christian post-secondary institution and Canada's largest seminary.[[220]](#cite_note-226)[[221]](#cite_note-227)[[222]](#cite_note-228)[[223]](#cite_note-229)

#### Universities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Universities")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/University_College_at_UofT%2C_October_10_2025_%2804%29.jpg/250px-University_College_at_UofT%2C_October_10_2025_%2804%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:University_College_at_UofT,_October_10_2025_(04).jpg)

[University College](/wiki/University_College,_Toronto "University College, Toronto") at the [University of Toronto](/wiki/University_of_Toronto "University of Toronto")'s St. George campus. University College is one of eleven constituent colleges at the University of Toronto.

Five [public universities](/wiki/Public_university "Public university") are based in Toronto. Four of these universities are based in downtown Toronto: [OCAD University](/wiki/OCAD_University "OCAD University"), [Toronto Metropolitan University](/wiki/Toronto_Metropolitan_University "Toronto Metropolitan University"), the [Université de l'Ontario français](/wiki/Universit%C3%A9_de_l%27Ontario_fran%C3%A7ais "Université de l'Ontario français"), and the main [St. George campus](/wiki/St._George_campus "St. George campus") of the [University of Toronto](/wiki/University_of_Toronto "University of Toronto").[[224]](#cite_note-230) The University of Toronto is the largest [post-secondary institution](/wiki/Higher_education_in_Canada "Higher education in Canada") in Canada and has three campuses, the other two of which are located in the city's eastern district of [Scarborough](/wiki/University_of_Toronto_Scarborough "University of Toronto Scarborough") and the neighbouring city of [Mississauga](/wiki/University_of_Toronto_Mississauga "University of Toronto Mississauga") respectively.[[225]](#cite_note-231) [York University](/wiki/York_University "York University") is the only Toronto-based university not situated in downtown Toronto, maintaining a [primary campus](/wiki/Keele_Campus "Keele Campus") in the northwestern portion of North York and a [secondary campus](/wiki/Glendon_College "Glendon College") in midtown Toronto.[[226]](#cite_note-232)

Several other public universities based elsewhere in Ontario also operate satellite campuses or facilities in Toronto, including [Queen's University at Kingston](/wiki/Queen%27s_University_at_Kingston "Queen's University at Kingston"), the [University of Ottawa](/wiki/University_of_Ottawa "University of Ottawa"), the [University of Western Ontario](/wiki/University_of_Western_Ontario "University of Western Ontario"), [Wilfrid Laurier University](/wiki/Wilfrid_Laurier_University "Wilfrid Laurier University"), and the [University of Guelph](/wiki/University_of_Guelph "University of Guelph").[[227]](#cite_note-ttceligsch-233) The latter operates a satellite campus in northwestern Etobicoke together with [Humber Polytechnic](/wiki/Humber_Polytechnic "Humber Polytechnic"), called the [University of Guelph-Humber](/wiki/University_of_Guelph-Humber "University of Guelph-Humber").[[228]](#cite_note-234) In addition to public universities, Toronto also holds a satellite campus for [Northeastern University](/wiki/Northeastern_University "Northeastern University"), a [private university](/wiki/Private_university "Private university") based in [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston").[[229]](#cite_note-235)

#### Colleges

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Colleges")]

There are four public [colleges](/wiki/College_(Canada) "College (Canada)") based in Toronto: [Centennial College](/wiki/Centennial_College_(Canada) "Centennial College (Canada)"), [George Brown College](/wiki/George_Brown_College "George Brown College"), Humber Polytechnic (formerly Humber College), and [Seneca Polytechnic](/wiki/Seneca_Polytechnic "Seneca Polytechnic") (formerly Seneca College). The four institutions operate several campuses throughout the city.[[230]](#cite_note-236) Several public colleges based elsewhere in Ontario also operate satellite facilities and campuses in Toronto, including [Cambrian College](/wiki/Cambrian_College "Cambrian College"), [Canadore College](/wiki/Canadore_College "Canadore College"), [Collège Boréal](/wiki/Coll%C3%A8ge_Bor%C3%A9al "Collège Boréal"), [Collège La Cité](/wiki/Coll%C3%A8ge_La_Cit%C3%A9 "Collège La Cité"), [Fleming College](/wiki/Fleming_College "Fleming College"), [Georgian College](/wiki/Georgian_College "Georgian College"), [Lambton College](/wiki/Lambton_College "Lambton College"), [Loyalist College](/wiki/Loyalist_College "Loyalist College"), [Niagara College](/wiki/Niagara_College "Niagara College"), [St. Clair College](/wiki/St._Clair_College "St. Clair College"), and [Sault College](/wiki/Sault_College "Sault College").[[227]](#cite_note-ttceligsch-233)

## Human resources

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Human resources")]

### Public health

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Public health")]

Main article: [Health in Toronto](/wiki/Health_in_Toronto "Health in Toronto")

See also: [List of hospitals in Toronto](/wiki/List_of_hospitals_in_Toronto "List of hospitals in Toronto")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/Toronto_-_ON_-_Toronto_General_Hospital.jpg/250px-Toronto_-_ON_-_Toronto_General_Hospital.jpg)](/wiki/File:Toronto_-_ON_-_Toronto_General_Hospital.jpg)

[Toronto General Hospital](/wiki/Toronto_General_Hospital "Toronto General Hospital") is a major [teaching hospital](/wiki/Teaching_hospital "Teaching hospital") in downtown Toronto

Toronto is home to twenty public hospitals, including [The Hospital for Sick Children](/wiki/The_Hospital_for_Sick_Children_(Toronto) "The Hospital for Sick Children (Toronto)"),[[231]](#cite_note-237) [Mount Sinai Hospital](/wiki/Mount_Sinai_Hospital_(Toronto) "Mount Sinai Hospital (Toronto)"),[[232]](#cite_note-238) [St. Michael's Hospital](/wiki/St._Michael%27s_Hospital_(Toronto) "St. Michael's Hospital (Toronto)"),[[233]](#cite_note-239) [North York General Hospital](/wiki/North_York_General_Hospital "North York General Hospital"),[[234]](#cite_note-240) [Toronto General Hospital](/wiki/Toronto_General_Hospital "Toronto General Hospital"), [Toronto Western Hospital](/wiki/Toronto_Western_Hospital "Toronto Western Hospital"), [Etobicoke General Hospital](/wiki/Etobicoke_General_Hospital "Etobicoke General Hospital"), [St. Joseph's Health Centre](/wiki/St._Joseph%27s_Health_Centre "St. Joseph's Health Centre"), [Scarborough General Hospital](/wiki/Scarborough_General_Hospital_(Toronto) "Scarborough General Hospital (Toronto)"), [Birchmount Hospital](/wiki/Birchmount_Hospital "Birchmount Hospital"), [Centenary Hospital](/wiki/Centenary_Hospital "Centenary Hospital"), [Sunnybrook Health Sciences Centre](/wiki/Sunnybrook_Health_Sciences_Centre "Sunnybrook Health Sciences Centre"), [Centre for Addiction and Mental Health](/wiki/Centre_for_Addiction_and_Mental_Health "Centre for Addiction and Mental Health") (CAMH), and [Princess Margaret Cancer Centre](/wiki/Princess_Margaret_Cancer_Centre "Princess Margaret Cancer Centre"), many of which are affiliated with the [Temerty Faculty of Medicine](/wiki/Temerty_Faculty_of_Medicine "Temerty Faculty of Medicine") of the [University of Toronto](/wiki/University_of_Toronto "University of Toronto").

Specialized hospitals include the [Baycrest Health Sciences](/wiki/Baycrest_Health_Sciences "Baycrest Health Sciences") geriatric hospital, the [Holland Bloorview Kids Rehabilitation Hospital](/wiki/Holland_Bloorview_Kids_Rehabilitation_Hospital "Holland Bloorview Kids Rehabilitation Hospital") for children with disabilities, and [Casey House](/wiki/Casey_House_(Toronto) "Casey House (Toronto)"), which cares for people living with, or at risk of contracting, AIDS.

Toronto's [Discovery District](/wiki/Discovery_District "Discovery District")[[235]](#cite_note-district-241) is a centre of research in [biomedicine](/wiki/Biomedicine "Biomedicine"). It is on a 2.5-square-kilometre (620-acre) research park that is integrated into Toronto's downtown core. It is also home to the [MaRS Discovery District](/wiki/MaRS_Discovery_District "MaRS Discovery District"),[[236]](#cite_note-mars-242) which was created in 2000 to capitalize on the research and innovation strength of the province of Ontario. Another institute is the McLaughlin Centre for Molecular Medicine (MCMM).[[237]](#cite_note-mole-243)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/MaRSfromCollege.jpg/250px-MaRSfromCollege.jpg)](/wiki/File:MaRSfromCollege.jpg)

MaRS Discovery District building at College Street. The organization is a medical research trust.

Toronto is also host to a wide variety of health-focused non-profit organizations that work to address specific illnesses for Toronto, Ontario and Canadian residents. Organizations include [Crohn's and Colitis Canada](/wiki/Crohn%27s_and_Colitis_Canada "Crohn's and Colitis Canada"), the [Heart and Stroke Foundation of Canada](/wiki/Heart_and_Stroke_Foundation_of_Canada "Heart and Stroke Foundation of Canada"), the [Canadian Cancer Society](/wiki/Canadian_Cancer_Society "Canadian Cancer Society"), the [Alzheimer Society of Canada](/wiki/Alzheimer_Society_of_Canada "Alzheimer Society of Canada"), and [Alzheimer Society of Ontario](/wiki/Alzheimer_Society_of_Ontario "Alzheimer Society of Ontario"), all located in the same office at [Yonge–Eglinton](/wiki/Yonge%E2%80%93Eglinton "Yonge–Eglinton"), the [Leukemia & Lymphoma Society of Canada](/wiki/Leukemia_%26_Lymphoma_Society_of_Canada "Leukemia & Lymphoma Society of Canada"), the [Canadian Breast Cancer Foundation](/wiki/Canadian_Breast_Cancer_Foundation "Canadian Breast Cancer Foundation"), the [Canadian Foundation for AIDS Research](/wiki/Canadian_Foundation_for_AIDS_Research "Canadian Foundation for AIDS Research"), [Cystic Fibrosis Canada](/wiki/Cystic_Fibrosis_Canada "Cystic Fibrosis Canada"), the [Canadian Mental Health Association](/wiki/Canadian_Mental_Health_Association "Canadian Mental Health Association"), and the [ALS Society of Canada](/wiki/ALS_Society_of_Canada "ALS Society of Canada").

In 2022, 187 homeless people died in Toronto, with 47 per cent dying of drug toxicity, the leading cause.[[238]](#cite_note-244) [Toronto Public Health](/wiki/Toronto_Public_Health "Toronto Public Health") described it as an "urgent public health issue", and has responded by opening supervised drug consumption sites, and by advocating for the allowance of personal drug possession.[[239]](#cite_note-245) In 2024 the Community Care and Recovery Act was passed by the Ontario government and Toronto Public Health was directed to close its [supervised consumption sites](/wiki/Supervised_injection_site "Supervised injection site"). As of April 1, 2025, Toronto Public Health no longer offers supervised consumption services. Some supervised consumption sites, not operated by Toronto Public Health, remain open.[[240]](#cite_note-246)

### Public library

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Public library")]

Main article: [Toronto Public Library](/wiki/Toronto_Public_Library "Toronto Public Library")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/TorontoReferenceLibrary8.jpg/250px-TorontoReferenceLibrary8.jpg)](/wiki/File:TorontoReferenceLibrary8.jpg)

The [Toronto Reference Library](/wiki/Toronto_Reference_Library "Toronto Reference Library"), the largest branch operated by [Toronto Public Library](/wiki/Toronto_Public_Library "Toronto Public Library") and the de facto main branch of the library system

Toronto Public Library is the largest public library system in Canada. In 2008, it averaged a higher circulation per capita than any other public library system internationally, making it the largest neighbourhood-based library system in the world.[[241]](#cite_note-247) Within North America, it also had the highest circulation and visitors when compared to other large urban systems.[[242]](#cite_note-An2009-248)

Established as the library of the [Mechanics' Institute](/wiki/Toronto_Mechanics%27_Institute "Toronto Mechanics' Institute") in 1830, the Toronto Public Library now consists of 100 branch libraries[[243]](#cite_note-100branches-249) and has over 12 million items in its collection.[[242]](#cite_note-An2009-248)[[244]](#cite_note-kupferman-250)[[245]](#cite_note-coll-251)[[246]](#cite_note-hist-252)

## Culture and contemporary life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Culture and contemporary life")]

Main article: [Culture in Toronto](/wiki/Culture_in_Toronto "Culture in Toronto")

See also: [Annual events in Toronto](/wiki/Annual_events_in_Toronto "Annual events in Toronto"), [List of festivals in Toronto](/wiki/List_of_festivals_in_Toronto "List of festivals in Toronto"), and [Recreation in Toronto](/wiki/Recreation_in_Toronto "Recreation in Toronto")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/TIFF_comes_in_Toronto_%2829465981882%29.jpg/250px-TIFF_comes_in_Toronto_%2829465981882%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:TIFF_comes_in_Toronto_(29465981882).jpg)

Crowds walk past the [Royal Alexandra Theatre](/wiki/Royal_Alexandra_Theatre "Royal Alexandra Theatre") during the [Toronto International Film Festival](/wiki/Toronto_International_Film_Festival "Toronto International Film Festival")

Toronto's theatre and [performing arts](/wiki/Performing_arts "Performing arts") scene has more than fifty ballet and dance companies, six opera companies, two symphony orchestras, [many music venues](/wiki/List_of_music_venues_in_Toronto "List of music venues in Toronto"), and a host of theatres. The city is home to the [National Ballet of Canada](/wiki/National_Ballet_of_Canada "National Ballet of Canada"),[[247]](#cite_note-253) the [Canadian Opera Company](/wiki/Canadian_Opera_Company "Canadian Opera Company"),[[248]](#cite_note-254) the [Toronto Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/Toronto_Symphony_Orchestra "Toronto Symphony Orchestra"),[[249]](#cite_note-255) the [Canadian Electronic Ensemble](/wiki/Canadian_Electronic_Ensemble "Canadian Electronic Ensemble"), and the [Canadian Stage Company](/wiki/Canadian_Stage_Company "Canadian Stage Company"). Notable performance venues include the [Four Seasons Centre for the Performing Arts](/wiki/Four_Seasons_Centre "Four Seasons Centre"), [Roy Thomson Hall](/wiki/Roy_Thomson_Hall "Roy Thomson Hall"), the [Princess of Wales Theatre](/wiki/Princess_of_Wales_Theatre "Princess of Wales Theatre"), the [Royal Alexandra Theatre](/wiki/Royal_Alexandra_Theatre "Royal Alexandra Theatre"), [Massey Hall](/wiki/Massey_Hall "Massey Hall"), the [Meridian Arts Centre](/wiki/Meridian_Arts_Centre "Meridian Arts Centre") (formerly the Toronto Centre for the Arts), the [Elgin and Winter Garden Theatres](/wiki/Elgin_and_Winter_Garden_Theatres "Elgin and Winter Garden Theatres"), and the [Meridian Hall](/wiki/Meridian_Hall_(Toronto) "Meridian Hall (Toronto)") (originally the "O'Keefe Centre" and formerly the "Hummingbird Centre" and the "Sony Centre for the Performing Arts").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/OntarioPlaceCinesphere.jpg/250px-OntarioPlaceCinesphere.jpg)](/wiki/File:OntarioPlaceCinesphere.jpg)

The [Cinesphere](/wiki/Cinesphere "Cinesphere") at [Ontario Place](/wiki/Ontario_Place "Ontario Place")

[Ontario Place](/wiki/Ontario_Place "Ontario Place") features the world's first permanent [IMAX](/wiki/IMAX "IMAX") movie theatre, the [Cinesphere](/wiki/Cinesphere "Cinesphere"),[[250]](#cite_note-256) as well as the [RBC Amphitheatre](/wiki/RBC_Amphitheatre "RBC Amphitheatre") (formerly Molson Amphitheatre and later Budweiser Stage), an open-air venue for music concerts. In the spring of 2012, Ontario Place closed after declining attendance. Although the RBC Amphitheatre and harbour still operate, the park and Cinesphere are no longer in use. There are ongoing plans to revitalise Ontario Place.[[251]](#cite_note-257)

[Rogers Stadium](/wiki/Rogers_Stadium_(Toronto) "Rogers Stadium (Toronto)") is a major concert venue located in [Downsview Park](/wiki/Downsview_Park "Downsview Park").

[Canada's Walk of Fame](/wiki/Canada%27s_Walk_of_Fame "Canada's Walk of Fame") acknowledges the achievements of successful Canadians with a series of stars on designated blocks of sidewalks along King Street and Simcoe Street.[[252]](#cite_note-258)

The production of domestic and foreign film and television is a major local industry. As of 2011, Toronto ranks as the third-largest production centre for film and television after [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles") and [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"),[[253]](#cite_note-259) sharing the nickname "[Hollywood North](/wiki/Hollywood_North "Hollywood North")" with Vancouver.[[254]](#cite_note-260)[[255]](#cite_note-Accessing_City_Hall-261)[[256]](#cite_note-262) The [Toronto International Film Festival](/wiki/Toronto_International_Film_Festival "Toronto International Film Festival") is an annual event celebrating the international film industry.[[257]](#cite_note-263)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/Caribana_2010.jpg/250px-Caribana_2010.jpg)](/wiki/File:Caribana_2010.jpg)

The grand parade for the [Caribana](/wiki/Caribana "Caribana") festival on [Lake Shore Boulevard](/wiki/Lake_Shore_Boulevard "Lake Shore Boulevard")

Toronto's [Caribana](/wiki/Caribana "Caribana") (formerly known as Scotiabank Caribbean Carnival) takes place from mid-July to early August of every summer.[[258]](#cite_note-Caribana-264) Primarily based on the [Trinidad and Tobago Carnival](/wiki/Trinidad_and_Tobago_Carnival "Trinidad and Tobago Carnival"), the first Caribana took place in 1967 when the city's Caribbean community celebrated [Canada's Centennial](/wiki/Canadian_Centennial "Canadian Centennial"). More than forty years later, it has grown to attract one million people to Toronto's [Lake Shore Boulevard](/wiki/Lake_Shore_Boulevard "Lake Shore Boulevard") annually. Tourism for the festival is in the hundreds of thousands, and each year, the event generates over $400 million in revenue for Ontario's economy.[[259]](#cite_note-265)

One of the most significant events in the city, [Pride Week](/wiki/Pride_Toronto "Pride Toronto"), takes place in late June and is one of the largest [LGBTQ](/wiki/LGBTQ "LGBTQ")+ festivals in the world.[[260]](#cite_note-266)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/Toronto_Island_Queen_City_Yacht_Club.jpg/250px-Toronto_Island_Queen_City_Yacht_Club.jpg)](/wiki/File:Toronto_Island_Queen_City_Yacht_Club.jpg)

Queen City Yacht Club facilities and piers on the Toronto Islands

Toronto is represented in five [major league sports](/wiki/Major_professional_sports_leagues_in_the_United_States_and_Canada "Major professional sports leagues in the United States and Canada"), with teams in the [National Hockey League](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League") (NHL), [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball") (MLB), [National Basketball Association](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association") (NBA), [Canadian Football League](/wiki/Canadian_Football_League "Canadian Football League") (CFL), and [Major League Soccer](/wiki/Major_League_Soccer "Major League Soccer") (MLS). It was formerly represented in a sixth and seventh; the [USL W-League](/wiki/USL_W-League_(1995%E2%80%932015) "USL W-League (1995–2015)") that announced on November 6, 2015, that it would cease operation ahead of the 2016 season and the [Canadian Women's Hockey League](/wiki/Canadian_Women%27s_Hockey_League "Canadian Women's Hockey League") ceased operations in May 2019.[[261]](#cite_note-267)[[262]](#cite_note-268)[[263]](#cite_note-269) The city's major sports venues include the [Scotiabank Arena](/wiki/Scotiabank_Arena "Scotiabank Arena") (formerly Air Canada Centre), [Rogers Centre](/wiki/Rogers_Centre "Rogers Centre") (formerly SkyDome), [Coca-Cola Coliseum](/wiki/Coca-Cola_Coliseum "Coca-Cola Coliseum") (formerly Ricoh Coliseum), and [BMO Field](/wiki/BMO_Field "BMO Field").

Historic sports clubs of Toronto include the [Granite Club](/wiki/Granite_Club "Granite Club") (established in 1836), the [Royal Canadian Yacht Club](/wiki/Royal_Canadian_Yacht_Club "Royal Canadian Yacht Club") (established in 1852), the [Toronto Cricket Skating and Curling Club](/wiki/Toronto_Cricket,_Skating_and_Curling_Club "Toronto Cricket, Skating and Curling Club") (established before 1827), the [Argonaut Rowing Club](/wiki/Argonaut_Rowing_Club "Argonaut Rowing Club") (established in 1872), the [Toronto Lawn Tennis Club](/wiki/Toronto_Lawn_Tennis_Club "Toronto Lawn Tennis Club") (established in 1881), and the Badminton and Racquet Club (established in 1924).[[264]](#cite_note-270)[[265]](#cite_note-271)

### Professional sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Professional sports")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/The_Blue_Jays_host_the_Orioles_in_the_AL_Wild_Card_Game_%2830243609331%29.jpg/250px-The_Blue_Jays_host_the_Orioles_in_the_AL_Wild_Card_Game_%2830243609331%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Blue_Jays_host_the_Orioles_in_the_AL_Wild_Card_Game_(30243609331).jpg)

The [2016 American League Wild Card Game](/wiki/2016_American_League_Wild_Card_Game "2016 American League Wild Card Game") played at [Rogers Centre](/wiki/Rogers_Centre "Rogers Centre"). The Toronto Blue Jays use the stadium.

Toronto is home to the [Toronto Maple Leafs](/wiki/Toronto_Maple_Leafs "Toronto Maple Leafs"), one of the NHL's [Original Six](/wiki/Original_Six "Original Six") clubs, and has also served as home to the Hockey Hall of Fame since 1958.[[266]](#cite_note-272) The city had a rich history of [hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey") championships. Along with the Maple Leafs' 13 [Stanley Cup](/wiki/Stanley_Cup "Stanley Cup") titles, the [Toronto Marlboros](/wiki/Toronto_Marlboros "Toronto Marlboros") and [St. Michael's College School](/wiki/St._Michael%27s_College_School "St. Michael's College School")-based [Ontario Hockey League](/wiki/Ontario_Hockey_League "Ontario Hockey League") teams, combined, have won a record 12 [Memorial Cup](/wiki/Memorial_Cup "Memorial Cup") titles.[[267]](#cite_note-273) The [Toronto Marlies](/wiki/Toronto_Marlies "Toronto Marlies") of the [American Hockey League](/wiki/American_Hockey_League "American Hockey League") also play in Toronto at Coca-Cola Coliseum and are the farm team for the Maple Leafs. The [Toronto Six](/wiki/Toronto_Six "Toronto Six"), the first Canadian franchise in the [National Women's Hockey League](/wiki/Premier_Hockey_Federation "Premier Hockey Federation"), began play with the 2020–21 season.[[268]](#cite_note-274) However, the National Women's Hockey League folded. Its successor, the [Professional Women's Hockey League](/wiki/Professional_Women%27s_Hockey_League "Professional Women's Hockey League"), has the [Toronto Sceptres](/wiki/Toronto_Sceptres "Toronto Sceptres").

The city is home to the [Toronto Blue Jays](/wiki/Toronto_Blue_Jays "Toronto Blue Jays") MLB baseball team. The team has won two [World Series](/wiki/World_Series "World Series") titles ([1992](/wiki/1992_World_Series "1992 World Series"), [1993](/wiki/1993_World_Series "1993 World Series")), though they lost the [2025 World Series](/wiki/2025_World_Series "2025 World Series").[[269]](#cite_note-275)[[270]](#cite_note-276) The Blue Jays play their home games at the Rogers Centre in the downtown core. Toronto has a long history of minor-league professional baseball dating back to the 1800s, culminating in the [Toronto Maple Leafs](/wiki/Toronto_Maple_Leafs_(International_League) "Toronto Maple Leafs (International League)") baseball team, whose owner first proposed an MLB team for Toronto.[[271]](#cite_note-277)

The [Toronto Raptors](/wiki/Toronto_Raptors "Toronto Raptors") basketball team entered the NBA in 1995 and has since earned eleven playoff spots and five [Atlantic Division](/wiki/Atlantic_Division_(NBA) "Atlantic Division (NBA)") titles in 24 seasons.[[272]](#cite_note-278) They won their first NBA title in [2019](/wiki/2019_NBA_Finals "2019 NBA Finals").[[273]](#cite_note-279) The Raptors are the only NBA team with their own television channel, [NBA TV Canada](/wiki/NBA_TV_Canada "NBA TV Canada").[[274]](#cite_note-280) They play their home games at Scotiabank Arena, which is shared with the Maple Leafs. In 2016, Toronto hosted the [65th NBA All-Star game](/wiki/2016_NBA_All-Star_Game "2016 NBA All-Star Game"), the first to be held outside the United States.[[275]](#cite_note-Toronto_hosts_2016_All_Star_game-281) The [Toronto Tempo](/wiki/Toronto_Tempo "Toronto Tempo") of the [Women's National Basketball Association](/wiki/Women%27s_National_Basketball_Association "Women's National Basketball Association") (WNBA) will begin play in 2026.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/Scotiabank_Arena_summer_2022.jpg/250px-Scotiabank_Arena_summer_2022.jpg)](/wiki/File:Scotiabank_Arena_summer_2022.jpg)

[Scotiabank Arena](/wiki/Scotiabank_Arena "Scotiabank Arena") from Bremner Boulevard. The [NBA](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association")'s Toronto Raptors and the [NHL](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League")'s Toronto Maple Leafs play their home games at the arena.

The city is represented in [Canadian football](/wiki/Canadian_football "Canadian football") by the CFL's [Toronto Argonauts](/wiki/Toronto_Argonauts "Toronto Argonauts"), which was founded in 1873.[[276]](#cite_note-282) The club has won 19 [Grey Cup](/wiki/Grey_Cup "Grey Cup") Canadian championship titles, with the most recent victory in 2024. The club's home games are played at BMO Field.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/Bmo_Field_2016_East_Stand.jpg/250px-Bmo_Field_2016_East_Stand.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bmo_Field_2016_East_Stand.jpg)

View of [BMO Field](/wiki/BMO_Field "BMO Field") from the grandstands. The [CFL](/wiki/Canadian_Football_League "Canadian Football League")'s Toronto Argonauts and [MLS](/wiki/Major_League_Soccer "Major League Soccer")' Toronto FC play their home games at the outdoor stadium.

Toronto is represented in soccer by the [Toronto FC](/wiki/Toronto_FC "Toronto FC") MLS team, who have won seven [Canadian Championship](/wiki/Canadian_Championship "Canadian Championship") titles, as well as the [MLS Cup](/wiki/MLS_Cup "MLS Cup") in [2017](/wiki/MLS_Cup_2017 "MLS Cup 2017") and the [Supporters' Shield](/wiki/Supporters%27_Shield "Supporters' Shield") for best regular season record, also in [2017](/wiki/2017_Major_League_Soccer_season "2017 Major League Soccer season").[[277]](#cite_note-283) They share BMO Field with the Toronto Argonauts. Toronto has a high level of participation in soccer across the city at several smaller stadiums and fields. Toronto FC entered the league as an expansion team in 2007.[[278]](#cite_note-284)[[279]](#cite_note-285) [AFC Toronto](/wiki/AFC_Toronto "AFC Toronto") of the [Northern Super League](/wiki/Northern_Super_League "Northern Super League") play at [York Lions Stadium](/wiki/York_Lions_Stadium "York Lions Stadium").

The [Toronto Rock](/wiki/Toronto_Rock "Toronto Rock") is the city's nominal [National Lacrosse League](/wiki/National_Lacrosse_League "National Lacrosse League") team. They won five [National Lacrosse League Cup](/wiki/National_Lacrosse_League_Cup "National Lacrosse League Cup") titles in seven years in the late 1990s and the first decade of the 21st century, appearing in an NLL-record five straight championship games from 1999 to 2003, and are first all-time in the number of Champion's Cups won. The Rock formerly shared the Scotiabank Arena with the Maple Leafs and the Raptors. However, the Toronto Rock moved to the nearby city of [Hamilton](/wiki/Hamilton,_Ontario "Hamilton, Ontario") while retaining its Toronto name.

The [Toronto Wolfpack](/wiki/Toronto_Wolfpack "Toronto Wolfpack") became Canada's first professional [rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league") team and the world's first transatlantic professional sports team when they began play in the [Rugby Football League](/wiki/Rugby_Football_League "Rugby Football League")'s [League One](/wiki/RFL_League_One "RFL League One") competition in 2017.[[280]](#cite_note-286) Due to COVID-19 restrictions on international travel the team withdrew from the [Super League](/wiki/Super_League "Super League") in 2020 with its future uncertain.[[281]](#cite_note-287) The rugby club's ownership changed in 2021, now 'Team Wolfpack' will play in the newly formed [North American Rugby League](/wiki/North_American_Rugby_League "North American Rugby League") tournament.[[282]](#cite_note-288)

Toronto is home to the [Toronto Rush](/wiki/Toronto_Rush "Toronto Rush"), a semi-professional ultimate team that competes in the [American Ultimate Disc League](/wiki/American_Ultimate_Disc_League "American Ultimate Disc League") (AUDL).[[283]](#cite_note-289)[[284]](#cite_note-290) [Ultimate (disc)](/wiki/Ultimate_frisbee "Ultimate frisbee"), in Canada, has its beginning roots in Toronto, with 3300 players competing annually in the Toronto Ultimate Club (League).[[285]](#cite_note-291)

Toronto has hosted several [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") (NFL) exhibition games at the Rogers Centre. [Ted Rogers](/wiki/Edward_S._Rogers_Jr. "Edward S. Rogers Jr.") [leased the Buffalo Bills](/wiki/Bills_Toronto_Series "Bills Toronto Series") from [Ralph Wilson](/wiki/Ralph_Wilson "Ralph Wilson") for the purposes of having the Bills play eight home games in the city between 2008 and 2013.
