### Collegiate sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Collegiate sports")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/2017_Red_and_Blue_Bowl.jpg/250px-2017_Red_and_Blue_Bowl.jpg)](/wiki/File:2017_Red_and_Blue_Bowl.jpg)

A Canadian football game between the [Toronto Varsity Blues](/wiki/Toronto_Varsity_Blues "Toronto Varsity Blues") and the [York Lions](/wiki/York_Lions "York Lions") at [York's Alumni Field](/wiki/Alumni_Field_(York_University) "Alumni Field (York University)")

The [University of Toronto](/wiki/University_of_Toronto "University of Toronto")'s [St. George campus](/wiki/St._George_campus "St. George campus") in downtown Toronto was where the first recorded [college football](/wiki/College_football "College football") game was held in November 1861.[[286]](#cite_note-292) Many post-secondary institutions in Toronto are members of [U Sports](/wiki/U_Sports "U Sports") or the [Canadian Collegiate Athletic Association](/wiki/Canadian_Collegiate_Athletic_Association "Canadian Collegiate Athletic Association"), the former for universities and the latter for colleges.

Toronto was home to the [International Bowl](/wiki/International_Bowl "International Bowl"), an [NCAA](/wiki/National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association "National Collegiate Athletic Association") sanctioned post-season college football game that pitted a [Mid-American Conference](/wiki/Mid-American_Conference "Mid-American Conference") team against a [Big East Conference](/wiki/Big_East_Conference_(1979%E2%80%932013) "Big East Conference (1979–2013)") team. From 2007 to 2010, the game was played at Rogers Centre annually in January.

### Events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Events")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/Queen_Elizabeth_at_the_Queen%27s_Plate.jpg/250px-Queen_Elizabeth_at_the_Queen%27s_Plate.jpg)](/wiki/File:Queen_Elizabeth_at_the_Queen%27s_Plate.jpg)

Arrival of [Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II") at the 2010 [Queen's Plate](/wiki/King%27s_Plate "King's Plate") at [Woodbine Racetrack](/wiki/Woodbine_Racetrack "Woodbine Racetrack")

Toronto, along with Montreal, hosts an annual [tennis](/wiki/Tennis "Tennis") tournament called the [Canadian Open](/wiki/Canadian_Open_(tennis) "Canadian Open (tennis)") (not to be confused with the [identically named golf tournament](/wiki/Canadian_Open_(golf) "Canadian Open (golf)")) between the months of July and August. In odd-numbered years, the men's tournament is held in Montreal, while the women's tournament is held in Toronto, and vice versa in even-numbered years.

The city hosts the [Toronto Waterfront Marathon](/wiki/Toronto_Waterfront_Marathon "Toronto Waterfront Marathon") annually, one of the [World Athletics Label Road Races](/wiki/World_Athletics_Label_Road_Races "World Athletics Label Road Races").[[287]](#cite_note-293) From 1986 to 2025, Toronto used to host the [Grand Prix of Toronto](/wiki/Grand_Prix_of_Toronto "Grand Prix of Toronto"), part of the [IndyCar Series](/wiki/IndyCar_Series "IndyCar Series") schedule, held on a street circuit at Exhibition Place [until it was moved to Markham](/wiki/IndyCar_Series_at_Markham_Centre "IndyCar Series at Markham Centre").[[288]](#cite_note-294) It was previously sanctioned by [Championship Auto Racing Teams (CART)](/wiki/Championship_Auto_Racing_Teams "Championship Auto Racing Teams") as the Molson Indy Toronto until 2007. Both [thoroughbred](/wiki/Thoroughbred "Thoroughbred") and [standardbred](/wiki/Standardbred "Standardbred") [horse racing](/wiki/Horse_racing "Horse racing") events are conducted at [Woodbine Racetrack](/wiki/Woodbine_Racetrack "Woodbine Racetrack") in [Rexdale](/wiki/Rexdale "Rexdale"), most notably the [King's Plate](/wiki/King%27s_Plate "King's Plate").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/2018_Honda_Indy_Toronto_at_Exhibition_Place.jpg/250px-2018_Honda_Indy_Toronto_at_Exhibition_Place.jpg)](/wiki/File:2018_Honda_Indy_Toronto_at_Exhibition_Place.jpg)

The 2018 [Grand Prix of Toronto](/wiki/Grand_Prix_of_Toronto "Grand Prix of Toronto"), an [IndyCar Series](/wiki/IndyCar_Series "IndyCar Series") race formerly held at [Exhibition Place](/wiki/Exhibition_Place "Exhibition Place")

Toronto hosted the [2015 Pan American Games](/wiki/2015_Pan_American_Games "2015 Pan American Games") in July 2015 and the [2015 Parapan American Games](/wiki/2015_Parapan_American_Games "2015 Parapan American Games") in August 2015. It beat the cities of [Lima](/wiki/Lima "Lima"), Peru, and [Bogotá](/wiki/Bogot%C3%A1 "Bogotá"), Colombia, to win the rights to stage the games.[[289]](#cite_note-295) The games were the largest [multi-sport event](/wiki/Multi-sport_event "Multi-sport event") ever to be held in Canada (in terms of athletes competing), double the size of the [2010 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2010_Winter_Olympics "2010 Winter Olympics") in [Vancouver](/wiki/Vancouver "Vancouver"), British Columbia.[[290]](#cite_note-296)

Toronto was a candidate city for the [1996](/wiki/1996_Summer_Olympics "1996 Summer Olympics") and [2008 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2008_Summer_Olympics "2008 Summer Olympics"), which were awarded to [Atlanta](/wiki/Atlanta "Atlanta") and [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing "Beijing") respectively.[[291]](#cite_note-297) The city previously hosted the [Summer Paralympics](/wiki/1976_Summer_Paralympics "1976 Summer Paralympics") in 1976.

Toronto was named as one of 16 cities in North America (and one of two Canadian cities) to host matches for the [2026 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2026_FIFA_World_Cup "2026 FIFA World Cup").[[292]](#cite_note-Toronto_selected_as_host_site_for_2026_FIFA_World_Cup_|_CTV_News-298)

## Government and politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Government and politics")]

Main article: [Municipal government of Toronto](/wiki/Municipal_government_of_Toronto "Municipal government of Toronto")

See also: [Politics of Toronto](/wiki/Politics_of_Toronto "Politics of Toronto") and [Public services in Toronto](/wiki/Public_services_in_Toronto "Public services in Toronto")

### Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Government")]

Toronto is a [single-tier municipality](/wiki/Single-tier_municipality "Single-tier municipality") governed by a [mayor–council system](/wiki/Mayor%E2%80%93council_government "Mayor–council government"). The structure of the municipal government is outlined the *[City of Toronto Act](/wiki/City_of_Toronto_Act "City of Toronto Act")*. The mayor of Toronto is elected by direct popular vote to serve as the [chief executive](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government") of the city. The [Toronto City Council](/wiki/Toronto_City_Council "Toronto City Council") is a [unicameral](/wiki/Unicameralism "Unicameralism") legislative body, comprising 25 councillors, since the [2018 municipal election](/wiki/2018_Toronto_municipal_election "2018 Toronto municipal election"), representing geographical [wards](/wiki/Ward_(electoral_subdivision) "Ward (electoral subdivision)") throughout the city.[[29]](#cite_note-:4-32)[[30]](#cite_note-Council_Members-33) The mayor and members of the city council serve four-year terms without [term limits](/wiki/Term_limit "Term limit"). (Until the [2006 municipal election](/wiki/2006_Toronto_municipal_election "2006 Toronto municipal election"), the mayor and city councillors served three-year terms.)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Cityhalltoronto1.JPG/250px-Cityhalltoronto1.JPG)](/wiki/File:Cityhalltoronto1.JPG)

[Toronto City Hall](/wiki/Toronto_City_Hall "Toronto City Hall") is the seat of the [municipal government of Toronto](/wiki/Municipal_government_of_Toronto "Municipal government of Toronto")

At the beginning of each term, mayor forms a striking committee which recommends the composition of other committees. The mayor appoints deputy mayors, committee chairs and vice chairs, and remaining committee members are appointed by council on the recommendation of the striking committee. An executive committee is formed by the chairs of each standing committee, the mayor, the deputy mayor and four other councillors. There are four standing committees, three special committees as well as other bodies which govern agencies such as the Board of Health, the Toronto Transit Commission Board and the [Toronto Police Service Board](/wiki/Toronto_Police_Services_Board "Toronto Police Services Board").[[293]](#cite_note-299)[[294]](#cite_note-300)

Administratively, the city is divided into four districts: North ([North York](/wiki/North_York "North York")), South ([Toronto](/wiki/Old_Toronto "Old Toronto") and [East York](/wiki/East_York "East York")), West ([Etobicoke](/wiki/Etobicoke "Etobicoke") and [York](/wiki/York,_Ontario "York, Ontario")) and East ([Scarborough](/wiki/Scarborough,_Ontario "Scarborough, Ontario")) City council has four community councils which have delegated decision-making authority on local, routine matters, and make recommendations to the city council on issues such as planning and zoning within their respective districts. Each city councillor is a member of the community council their ward is in.[[295]](#cite_note-TCC-301)

There are about 40 subcommittees and advisory committees appointed by the city council. These bodies are made up of city councillors and private citizen volunteers. Examples include the Pedestrian Committee, Waste Diversion Task Force 2010, and the [Task Force to Bring Back the Don](/wiki/Task_Force_to_Bring_Back_the_Don "Task Force to Bring Back the Don").[[296]](#cite_note-302)

The City of Toronto had an approved operating budget of CA$13.53 billion in 2020 and a ten-year capital budget and plan of CA$43.5 billion.[[297]](#cite_note-303) The city's revenues include subsidies from the [Government of Canada](/wiki/Government_of_Canada "Government of Canada") and the Government of Ontario (for programs mandated by those governments), 33 per cent from property tax, 6 per cent from the land transfer tax and the rest from other tax revenues and user fees.[[298]](#cite_note-cot-chart-304) The city's largest operating expenditures are the Toronto Transit Commission at CA$2.14 billion,[[299]](#cite_note-305) and the [Toronto Police Service](/wiki/Toronto_Police_Service "Toronto Police Service"), CA$1.22 billion.[[300]](#cite_note-306)

### Crime

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Crime")]

Main article: [Crime in Toronto](/wiki/Crime_in_Toronto "Crime in Toronto")

See also: [Crime in Canada](/wiki/Crime_in_Canada "Crime in Canada") and [Gun politics in Canada](/wiki/Gun_politics_in_Canada "Gun politics in Canada")

The historically low [crime rate](/wiki/Crime_statistics "Crime statistics") in Toronto has resulted in the city having a reputation as one of the safest major cities in North America.[[301]](#cite_note-crime-307)[[302]](#cite_note-crime1-308)[[303]](#cite_note-crime2-309) For instance, in 2007, the [homicide](/wiki/Homicide "Homicide") rate for Toronto was 3.43 per 100,000 people, compared with Atlanta (19.7), Boston (10.3), Los Angeles (10.0), New York City (6.3), Vancouver (3.1), and Montreal (2.6). Toronto's robbery rate also ranks low, with 207.1 robberies per 100,000 people, compared with Los Angeles (348.5), Vancouver (266.2), New York City (265.9), and Montreal (235.3).[[304]](#cite_note-310)[[305]](#cite_note-311)[[306]](#cite_note-312)[[307]](#cite_note-313)[[308]](#cite_note-314)[[309]](#cite_note-315)[*[excessive citations](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#Bundling_citations "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] Toronto has a comparable rate of [car theft](/wiki/Motor_vehicle_theft "Motor vehicle theft") to various U.S. cities, although it is not among the highest in Canada.[[301]](#cite_note-crime-307)

In 2005, Toronto media coined the term "Year of the Gun" because of a record number of gun-related homicides, 52 out of 80 homicides in total.[[303]](#cite_note-crime2-309)[[310]](#cite_note-316) The total number of homicides dropped to 70 in 2006; that year, nearly 2,000 people in Toronto were victims of a violent gun-related crime, about one-quarter of the national total.[[311]](#cite_note-317) 86 homicides were committed in 2007, roughly half of which involved guns. Gang-related incidents have also been on the rise; between 1997 and 2005, over 300 gang-related homicides have occurred. As a result, the Ontario government developed an anti-gun strategy.[[312]](#cite_note-318)[[313]](#cite_note-319) In 2011, Toronto's murder rate plummeted to 51 murders—nearly a 26% drop from the previous year. The 51 homicides were the lowest number the city has recorded since 1999 when there were 47.[[314]](#cite_note-320) While subsequent years did see a return to higher rates, it remained nearly flat line of 57–59 homicides in from 2012 to 2015. 2016 went to 75 for the first time in over eight years. 2017 had a drop off of 10 murders to close the year at 65, with a homicide rate of 2.4 per 100,000 population.[[315]](#cite_note-TPS2015YTD-321)

The total number of homicides in Toronto reached a record 98 in 2018; the number included 14 fatalities from the [Toronto van attack](/wiki/2018_Toronto_van_attack "2018 Toronto van attack") and the [Danforth shooting](/wiki/2018_Toronto_shooting "2018 Toronto shooting"), which gave the city a homicide rate of around 3.6 per 100,000 people. The record year for murders was previously 1991, with 89, at a rate of 3.9 murders per 100,000 people.[[316]](#cite_note-322)[[317]](#cite_note-Major_Crime_Indicators-323) The 2018 homicide rate was higher than in [Winnipeg](/wiki/Winnipeg "Winnipeg"), [Calgary](/wiki/Calgary "Calgary"), [Edmonton](/wiki/Edmonton "Edmonton"), Vancouver, Ottawa, Montreal, [Hamilton](/wiki/Hamilton,_Ontario "Hamilton, Ontario"), New York City, [San Diego](/wiki/San_Diego "San Diego"), and [Austin](/wiki/Austin,_Texas "Austin, Texas").[[318]](#cite_note-324) Homicides in 2019 dropped to 80 (a rate of 2.9 per 100,000 people) [below the rate of most US cities](/wiki/List_of_United_States_cities_by_crime_rate "List of United States cities by crime rate"), but still higher than the Canadian average of 1.8.[[319]](#cite_note-325) Shooting incidents also increased to an all-time high of 492 in 2019, even outpacing gun incidents that occurred in 2018.[[320]](#cite_note-326) 2020 saw another decrease in homicides with the city having a total of 71 murders for the year (a rate of around 2.6 per 100,000 people).[[321]](#cite_note-327) However, in 2021, the city saw an increase in homicides, with the city murders increasing to 85, giving Toronto a homicide rate of 3.04 per 100,000 people.[[322]](#cite_note-328)[[323]](#cite_note-329) A decrease in murders happened the following year with 71 being reported in 2022 (a murder rate of 2.5 per 100,000), which was then followed by a slight increase in homicides with 73 being reported in 2023, giving the city a murder rate of 2.6 per 100,000 people, along with a record 12,143 reports of auto theft in the year.[[317]](#cite_note-Major_Crime_Indicators-323) 2024 saw another increase in homicides with 85 being reported in the year, giving the city a homicide rate of around 3.04 per 100,000 people.[[324]](#cite_note-330)[[325]](#cite_note-331)

## Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Transportation")]

Main articles: [Transportation in Toronto](/wiki/Transportation_in_Toronto "Transportation in Toronto") and [Public transportation in Toronto](/wiki/Public_transportation_in_Toronto "Public transportation in Toronto")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/Toronto_Bike_Lanes_%28Sherbourne_Street%29_Bandes_cyclables_de_Toronto_%28rue_Sherbourne%29_%2838301287985%29.jpg/250px-Toronto_Bike_Lanes_%28Sherbourne_Street%29_Bandes_cyclables_de_Toronto_%28rue_Sherbourne%29_%2838301287985%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Toronto_Bike_Lanes_(Sherbourne_Street)_Bandes_cyclables_de_Toronto_(rue_Sherbourne)_(38301287985).jpg)

A roadway with [bike lanes](/wiki/Bike_lane "Bike lane"). A [public bus service](/wiki/Toronto_Transit_Commission_bus_system "Toronto Transit Commission bus system") operated by the [Toronto Transit Commission](/wiki/Toronto_Transit_Commission "Toronto Transit Commission") is visible in the background.

Toronto is a central transportation hub for road, rail, and air networks in Southern Ontario. The city has many forms of transport, including [highways](/wiki/400-series_highways "400-series highways") and [public transit](/wiki/Public_transportation_in_Toronto "Public transportation in Toronto"). The [Toronto Transit Commission](/wiki/Toronto_Transit_Commission "Toronto Transit Commission") (TTC) is the main operator of public transportation in the city. Toronto also has an extensive [network of bicycle lanes](/wiki/Cycling_in_Toronto "Cycling in Toronto") and multi-use trails and paths.

### Rail

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Rail")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/TTC_Rosedale_Station_in_Toronto_-_%2820180909173532%29.jpg/250px-TTC_Rosedale_Station_in_Toronto_-_%2820180909173532%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:TTC_Rosedale_Station_in_Toronto_-_(20180909173532).jpg)

A [TTC subway](/wiki/Toronto_subway "Toronto subway") for [Line 1 Yonge–University](/wiki/Line_1_Yonge%E2%80%93University "Line 1 Yonge–University") at [Rosedale station](/wiki/Rosedale_station_(Toronto) "Rosedale station (Toronto)")

Toronto has various forms of rail transport, including [streetcars](/wiki/Streetcar "Streetcar"), [light rail](/wiki/Light_rail "Light rail"), [heavy rail](/wiki/Heavy_rail "Heavy rail"), [regional rail](/wiki/Regional_rail "Regional rail"), and [inter-city](/wiki/Inter-city_rail "Inter-city rail") passenger and [freight rail](/wiki/Freight_rail "Freight rail"). The backbone of its public transport network is the [Toronto subway](/wiki/Toronto_subway "Toronto subway"), a system of underground, surface, and [elevated railways](/wiki/Elevated_railway "Elevated railway") operated by the TTC. The Toronto subway consists of three heavy rail rapid transit lines spanning the city, the U-shaped [Line 1](/wiki/Line_1_Yonge%E2%80%93University "Line 1 Yonge–University"), east–west [Line 2](/wiki/Line_2_Bloor%E2%80%93Danforth "Line 2 Bloor–Danforth") and the short east–west [Line 4](/wiki/Line_4_Sheppard "Line 4 Sheppard"), and two light rail lines, [Line 5](/wiki/Line_5_Eglinton "Line 5 Eglinton") and [Line 6](/wiki/Line_6_Finch_West "Line 6 Finch West"), both running east–west.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/Flexity_Outlook_%28Toronto_streetcar%29%3B_September_2019.jpg/250px-Flexity_Outlook_%28Toronto_streetcar%29%3B_September_2019.jpg)](/wiki/File:Flexity_Outlook_(Toronto_streetcar);_September_2019.jpg)

A [TTC streetcar](/wiki/Toronto_streetcar_system "Toronto streetcar system") on King Street. The streetcar system is the largest and busiest such system in North America.

The TTC also operates a network of [streetcars](/wiki/Toronto_streetcar_system "Toronto streetcar system") primarily serving streets in downtown that do not have subway service.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/Skyscrapers_by_railway_station_%28Unsplash%29.jpg/250px-Skyscrapers_by_railway_station_%28Unsplash%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Skyscrapers_by_railway_station_(Unsplash).jpg)

The [Union Station Rail Corridor](/wiki/Union_Station_Rail_Corridor "Union Station Rail Corridor") at [Union Station](/wiki/Union_Station_(Toronto) "Union Station (Toronto)"). The corridor is used by [commuter](/wiki/Commuter_rail "Commuter rail") and [intercity rail](/wiki/Intercity_rail "Intercity rail") services.

[Union Station](/wiki/Union_Station_(Toronto) "Union Station (Toronto)"), located in downtown, is the city's main railway station and hub for regional and inter-city rail, and is serviced by the subway and streetcars with [an adjacent station of the same name](/wiki/Union_station_(TTC) "Union station (TTC)"). [GO Transit](/wiki/GO_Transit "GO Transit"), owned by the provincial government, operates [a regional rail network](/wiki/GO_Transit_rail_services "GO Transit rail services") centred on Union Station that connects downtown Toronto with the [Greater Golden Horseshoe](/wiki/Greater_Golden_Horseshoe "Greater Golden Horseshoe") region. GO Transit carries over 250,000 passengers every weekday (2013) and 57 million annually, with a majority of them travelling to or from Union Station.[[326]](#cite_note-GO_Numbers-332)[[327]](#cite_note-333) [Metrolinx](/wiki/Metrolinx "Metrolinx") is currently implementing [Regional Express Rail](/wiki/GO_Transit_Regional_Express_Rail "GO Transit Regional Express Rail") into its GO Transit network and plans to electrify many of its rail lines by 2030.[[328]](#cite_note-334) [Via Rail](/wiki/Via_Rail "Via Rail"), Canada's federally owned inter-city rail network, connects Toronto with other cities in the heart of [Central Canada](/wiki/Central_Canada "Central Canada") with the [Quebec City–Windsor Corridor](/wiki/Quebec_City%E2%80%93Windsor_Corridor_(Via_Rail) "Quebec City–Windsor Corridor (Via Rail)"), and with [Northern Ontario](/wiki/Northern_Ontario "Northern Ontario") and [Western Canada](/wiki/Western_Canada "Western Canada") with *[The Canadian](/wiki/The_Canadian "The Canadian")*, a trans-continental rail service. The *[Maple Leaf](/wiki/Maple_Leaf_(train) "Maple Leaf (train)")*, jointly operated by Via Rail and [Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak"), runs between Toronto and [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), and is the only international rail service to Toronto.[[329]](#cite_note-335)

There have been numerous plans to extend the subway and implement [light rail](/wiki/Light_rail "Light rail") lines, but budgetary concerns have thwarted many efforts. By November 2011, construction on [Line 5 Eglinton](/wiki/Line_5_Eglinton "Line 5 Eglinton"), an east–west light rail line, began. Line 5 originally scheduled to finish construction by 2020, but was delayed several times to 2026.[[330]](#cite_note-336) The [Line 6 Finch West](/wiki/Line_6_Finch_West "Line 6 Finch West") light rail line opened in 2025. In 2019, the Government of Ontario released a transit plan for the Greater Toronto Area which includes a new 16-kilometre (9.9 mi) [Ontario Line](/wiki/Ontario_Line "Ontario Line"),[[331]](#cite_note-337) Line 1 extension to [Richmond Hill Centre](/wiki/Richmond_Hill_Centre_Terminal "Richmond Hill Centre Terminal"),[[332]](#cite_note-338) a Line 2 extension to Sheppard Avenue / McCowan Road to replace [Line 3](/wiki/Line_3_Scarborough "Line 3 Scarborough"), and an extension for Line 5 Eglinton to Toronto Pearson International Airport.[[333]](#cite_note-339)[[334]](#cite_note-340)

Toronto's century-old [Union Station](/wiki/Union_Station_(Toronto) "Union Station (Toronto)") is also getting a major renovation and upgrade which would be able to accommodate more rail traffic from [GO Transit](/wiki/GO_Transit_rail_services "GO Transit rail services"), [Via Rail](/wiki/Via_Rail "Via Rail"), [UP Express](/wiki/Union_Pearson_Express "Union Pearson Express") and [Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak").[[335]](#cite_note-341) Construction on a new [Union Station Bus Terminal](/wiki/Union_Station_Bus_Terminal "Union Station Bus Terminal") began in 2017 and was completed in 2020. In addition, the [East Harbour Transit Hub](/wiki/East_Harbour_Transit_Hub "East Harbour Transit Hub") is slated to open in 2028.[[336]](#cite_note-342) Toronto's public transit network also connects to other municipal networks such as [York Region Transit](/wiki/York_Region_Transit "York Region Transit"), [Viva](/wiki/Viva_Rapid_Transit "Viva Rapid Transit"), [Durham Region Transit](/wiki/Durham_Region_Transit "Durham Region Transit"), [Brampton Transit](/wiki/Brampton_Transit "Brampton Transit"), and [MiWay](/wiki/MiWay "MiWay").

### Bus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Bus")]

The TTC operates an extensive [bus network](/wiki/Toronto_Transit_Commission_bus_system "Toronto Transit Commission bus system") that serves parts of the city many not served by the sparse subway network. The TTC bus system had an annual ridership of over 389 million trips in 2024.[[337]](#cite_note-APTA_Ridership_Report-343)

GO Transit provides intercity bus services from the Union Station Bus Terminal and other bus terminals in the city to destinations within the Golden Horseshoe. Long-distance [intercity coach services](/wiki/Intercity_coach_service "Intercity coach service") by multiple companies also operated from the Union Station Bus Terminal and provide a network of services to further cities in Ontario, neighbouring provinces, and the United States. The [Toronto Coach Terminal](/wiki/Toronto_Coach_Terminal "Toronto Coach Terminal") formerly served as the city's intercity coach hub from 1931 to 2021, when the terminal was decommissioned.[[338]](#cite_note-344)

### Sea

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Sea")]

The [Port of Toronto](/wiki/Port_of_Toronto "Port of Toronto") in the [Toronto Harbour](/wiki/Toronto_Harbour "Toronto Harbour") receives 2 million tons of cargo annually as of 2024.[[339]](#cite_note-345)

The [Toronto Island ferries](/wiki/Toronto_Island_ferries "Toronto Island ferries") travel from ferry terminals in the downtown mainland to the [Toronto Islands](/wiki/Toronto_Islands "Toronto Islands"). Ferries operated by the city carried over 1.4 million passengers in 2024.[[340]](#cite_note-346)

### Air

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto&action=edit&section=49 "Edit section: Air")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/Pearson_International.JPG/250px-Pearson_International.JPG)](/wiki/File:Pearson_International.JPG)

Interior of [Toronto Pearson International Airport](/wiki/Toronto_Pearson_International_Airport "Toronto Pearson International Airport")'s Terminal 1. Toronto Pearson serves as the [international airport](/wiki/International_airport "International airport") for the [Greater Toronto Area](/wiki/Greater_Toronto_Area "Greater Toronto Area")

Canada's busiest airport, [Toronto Pearson International Airport](/wiki/Toronto_Pearson_International_Airport "Toronto Pearson International Airport") ([IATA](/wiki/IATA_airport_code "IATA airport code"): YYZ), straddles the city's western boundary with the suburban city of Mississauga. The Union Pearson Express (UP Express) train service provides a direct link between Pearson International and Union Station. It began carrying passengers in June 2015.[[341]](#cite_note-347)

Limited commercial and passenger service to nearby destinations in Canada and the United States is offered from the [Billy Bishop Toronto City Airport](/wiki/Billy_Bishop_Toronto_City_Airport "Billy Bishop Toronto City Airport") (IATA: YTZ) on the Toronto Islands, southwest of downtown. [Downsview Airport](/wiki/Downsview_Airport "Downsview Airport") (IATA: YZD), was located near the city's north end, and was owned by [de Havilland Canada](/wiki/De_Havilland_Canada_(1928%E2%80%931986) "De Havilland Canada (1928–1986)") serving as the [Bombardier Aviation](/wiki/Bombardier_Aviation "Bombardier Aviation") aircraft factory. The airport permanently ceased operations in April 2024.[[342]](#cite_note-348)

Within a few hours' drive, Hamilton's [John C. Munro International Airport](/wiki/John_C._Munro_Hamilton_International_Airport "John C. Munro Hamilton International Airport") (IATA: YHM) and Buffalo's [Buffalo Niagara International Airport](/wiki/Buffalo_Niagara_International_Airport "Buffalo Niagara International Airport") (IATA: BUF) serve as alternate airports for the Toronto area in addition to serving their respective cities. A [secondary international airport](/wiki/Pickering_Airport_Lands "Pickering Airport Lands"), to be located northeast of Toronto in [Pickering](/wiki/Pickering,_Ontario "Pickering, Ontario"), had been planned by the Government of Canada, but was permanently cancelled due to the Alto high-speed rail project, which would make the airport no longer necessary.

The city is also serviced by a [number of private heliports](/wiki/List_of_airports_in_the_Greater_Toronto_Area "List of airports in the Greater Toronto Area"), most of which are attached to local hospitals and exclusively used for [medevac](/wiki/Medical_evacuation "Medical evacuation") flights.[[343]](#cite_note-349)

### Streets and highways

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto&action=edit&section=50 "Edit section: Streets and highways")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/Highway_401_Densification.jpg/250px-Highway_401_Densification.jpg)](/wiki/File:Highway_401_Densification.jpg)

[Highway 401](/wiki/Ontario_Highway_401 "Ontario Highway 401") is a [400-series highway](/wiki/400-series_highways "400-series highways") that passes west to east through Greater Toronto. Toronto's portion of Highway 401 is the busiest highway in North America.

The grid of major city streets was laid out by a [concession road](/wiki/Concession_road "Concession road") system, in which major [arterial roads](/wiki/Arterial_road "Arterial road") are 6,600 ft (2.0 km) apart (with some exceptions, particularly in Scarborough and Etobicoke, as they used a different survey). Major east-west arterial roads are generally parallel with the Lake Ontario shoreline, and major north–south arterial roads are roughly perpendicular to the shoreline, though slightly angled north of Eglinton Avenue. This arrangement is sometimes broken by geographical accidents, most notably the Don River ravines. Toronto's grid north is approximately 18.5° to the west of true north. Many arterials, particularly north–south ones, due to the city originally being within the former [York County](/wiki/York_County,_Ontario "York County, Ontario"), continue beyond the city into the [905 suburbs](/wiki/Area_code_905 "Area code 905") and further into the rural countryside.

There are several municipal [expressways](/wiki/Limited-access_road "Limited-access road") and [provincial highways](/wiki/Ontario_Provincial_Highway_Network "Ontario Provincial Highway Network") that serve Toronto and the Greater Toronto Area. In particular, [Highway 401](/wiki/Ontario_Highway_401 "Ontario Highway 401") bisects the city from west to east, bypassing the downtown core. It is the busiest road in North America,[[344]](#cite_note-fhwa-350)
and one of the busiest highways in the world.[[345]](#cite_note-351)[[346]](#cite_note-352) Other provincial highways include [Highway 400](/wiki/Ontario_Highway_400 "Ontario Highway 400"), which connects the city with Northern Ontario and beyond and [Highway 404](/wiki/Ontario_Highway_404 "Ontario Highway 404"), an extension of the [Don Valley Parkway](/wiki/Don_Valley_Parkway "Don Valley Parkway") into the northern suburbs. The [Queen Elizabeth Way](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth_Way "Queen Elizabeth Way") (QEW), North America's first divided intercity highway, terminates at Toronto's western boundary and connects Toronto to [Niagara Falls](/wiki/Niagara_Falls "Niagara Falls") and [Buffalo](/wiki/Buffalo,_New_York "Buffalo, New York"). The main municipal expressways in Toronto include the [Gardiner Expressway](/wiki/Gardiner_Expressway "Gardiner Expressway"), the Don Valley Parkway, and, to some extent, [Allen Road](/wiki/Allen_Road "Allen Road"). Toronto's traffic congestion is one of the highest in North America, and is the second highest in Canada after Vancouver.[[347]](#cite_note-353)
